subject: Science
q: Gender is an example of a _____ trait.
skill: Define variation and discuss its types
feedback: Gender is an example of a discontinuously variable trait. A person can either be male or female.

subject: Science
q: The frequency(occurrence) of a trait is represented by the graph shown below. What does the graph suggest about this trait?
skill: Define variation and discuss its types
feedback: The graph shows that the trait shows 4 variations (A, B, C and D) which show different frequencies. This indicates that the trait presents itself in distinctly different variations and therefore is discontinuously variable. The trait is actually blood group, where the A, B, C and D are blood type A, B, AB, and O respectively. A continuously variable trait, such as height would be present in a range of measurements and would show a normal distribution.

subject: Science
q: Which of these traits does NOT show continuous variation?
skill: Define variation and discuss its types
feedback: Blood group is NOT an example of a continuously variable trait since it can only consist of four distinctly different variations, .i.e. blood type A, B, AB or O. There is no range of values which exists in blood types unlike in height, hand size, finger length etc. Therefore, blood group is discontinuously variable.

subject: Science
q: Foot size is an example of a _____ trait.
skill: Define variation and discuss its types
feedback: Foot size is an example of a continuously variable trait, because foot size can be anywhere between a wide range of measurements and does not show distinctly different variations.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following statements correctly explains why eye colour shows discontinuous variation?
options: It is affected by the lifestyle choices of a person.
It can fit anywhere within a range of measurements.
It is a distinctly different variability.
skill: Define variation and discuss its types
feedback: Eye colour is an example of a trait with discontinuous variation because it shows distinctly different variability. A person can either have blue eyes or brown eyes, there is no in between.

subject: Science
q: Which of these traits shows acquired variation?
skill: Define variation and discuss its types
feedback: Acquired variation is shown by traits which are significantly affected by a person's activities/lifestyle. So, for example, eye colour is NOT an example of acquired variation, since a person's eye colour does not change depending on their activities. However, a person's weight is affected by how much they eat, how much they exercise and so on. So, weight is an example of a trait that shows acquired variation.

subject: Science
q: Which of these traits is presented by the graph below?
skill: Define variation and discuss its types
feedback: The graph depicts a bell curve, which is seen whenever a variable is normally distributed i.e. consists of roughly symmetrical distributed data. Only continuous variables i.e. which can vary within a wide range of measurements, show normal distribution. Whenever a continuous variable is measured, it will roughly assume the shape of a bell curve, it will consist of a maximum/minimum value and a mean value. Out of the options, only height is a continuous variable, therefore the graph is representing heights.

subject: Science
q: A strand of DNA with the sequence AACTTG will have a complimentary strand with the sequence _____.
skill: Describe the structure and function of chromosomes and DNA in an animal cell
feedback:
The nitrogenous bases form hydrogen bonds between opposing DNA strands to form the rungs of the twisted ladder or double helix of the DNA.
Adenine is always paired with thymine, and guanine is always paired with cytosine. So, a strand of DNA with the sequence AACTTG will have a complimentary strand with the sequence TTGAAC.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following is considered the basic unit of heredity?
skill: Describe the structure and function of chromosomes and DNA in an animal cell
feedback: A gene is the basic unit through which genetic information is passed down from parents to children. Each gene occupies a specific position on a chromosome. It helps determine the different characteristics or traits of an individual, such as the shape of one's nose or the colour of one's skin.

subject: Science
q: DNA looks like a <u>twisted ladder</u>.  If the <u>rungs</u> of the ladder are made up of nitrogenous bases, what are the hand rails or backbone of the ladder made up of?
skill: Describe the structure and function of chromosomes and DNA in an animal cell
feedback:
Each nucleotide consists of three components, a nitrogenous base, a five-carbon sugar molecule called deoxyribose, and a phosphate molecule.
Hence, sugar and phosphate molecules make up the backbone of a DNA molecule.

subject: Science
q: DNA is made up of nitrogenous base pairs.  Which pattern do the bases bond in?
skill: Describe the structure and function of chromosomes and DNA in an animal cell
feedback: The nitrogenous bases always exist in pairs. Adenine is attached with thymine, while guanine always pairs up with cytosine.

subject: Science
q: How many nitrogenous bases are there in DNA?
skill: Describe the structure and function of chromosomes and DNA in an animal cell
feedback: There are four nitrogenous bases in a nucleotide of DNA namely adenine, thymine, guanine, and cytosine.

subject: Science
q: Each nucleotide consists of a nitrogenous base, a _____, and a _____ molecule.
skill: Describe the structure and function of chromosomes and DNA in an animal cell
feedback: Each nucleotide consists of three components, a nitrogenous base, a five-carbon sugar molecule called deoxyribose, and a phosphate molecule.

subject: Science
q: The unit of a DNA molecule is called a _____.
skill: Describe the structure and function of chromosomes and DNA in an animal cell
feedback: A DNA molecule is made up of a very large number of units called nucleotides.

subject: Science
q: Chromosomes and genes are made up of
skill: Describe the structure and function of chromosomes and DNA in an animal cell
feedback: Chromosomes and genes are made up of a molecule known as deoxyribose nucleic acid or DNA. The DNA molecule is composed of two chains that twist around each other to form a double helix.

subject: Science
q: The skin cells of an animal contain 8 chromosomes. How many chromosomes will be present in each of the gametes produced by this animal?
skill: Define heredity and recognise its importance in transferring characteristics
feedback:
Gametes contain half the chromosomes contained in normal diploid cells of the body, which are also known as somatic cells.
Haploid gametes are produced during meiosis, which is a type of cell division that reduces the number of chromosomes in a parent diploid cell by half.
So, if the animal's skin cells contain 8 chromosomes, the total number of chromosomes present in gametes will be half, which is equal to 4.

subject: Science
q: The earlobes that hang below the point of attachment to the head are called
skill: Define heredity and recognise its importance in transferring characteristics
feedback:
Earlobe attachment is one of the many inherited characteristics in humans. If the earlobes do not hang, they are called attached earlobes.
If earlobes hang free, they are detached and are called free earlobes.

subject: Science
q: Which one is the most common and least common eye colour?
options: most common is brown, least common is blue
most common is black, least common is green
most common is brown, least common is green
most common is black, least common is blue
skill: Define heredity and recognise its importance in transferring characteristics
feedback:
The most common eye colour is brown and least common is blue.

More than 55% of the world's population have brown eyes and less than 10% of the world's population have blue coloured eyes. In blue eyes, the amount of pigment is very low and the light scattering effect gives off a blue colour. The same phenomenon gives the sky its blue colour.

subject: Science
q: The variations in eye colour are attributed to the presence of varying ratios of _____ in the iris which is controlled by a pair of genes.
skill: Define heredity and recognise its importance in transferring characteristics
feedback: Human eye colour is determined by the amount of pigment in the iris and the way the iris scatters the light passing through it. Genes dictate how much pigmentation will be present in the eye. The more the pigment, the darker the eye colour.

subject: Science
q: Differences among members of a family or a species are called
skill: Define heredity and recognise its importance in transferring characteristics
feedback: Variations are also known as genetic diversity or differences within a species. Variations may be defined as the differences among the individual of the same species.

subject: Science
q: The shape of DNA is also called a double helix because it is made up of
options: two straight strands running parallel to each other.
a single strand curved in a helical shape.
skill: Define heredity and recognise its importance in transferring characteristics
feedback:
The DNA is called a double helix structure. Double means two strands of DNA and helix means a twisted or curved structure.
Therefore, DNA is made up of two strands twisted together in a double helix.

subject: Science
q:
The diagram shows different types of earlobes:

One type of earlobes attaches directly to the side of the head.Which image(s) suits best the given description?
skill: Define heredity and recognise its importance in transferring characteristics
feedback:
Attached earlobes B are small in size and do not hang. They are attached directly to the side of the head.
A and C show detached or free ear lobe.

subject: Science
q:
The diagram shows chromosomes of an organism.

These are the chromosomes of
skill: Define heredity and recognise its importance in transferring characteristics
feedback: The diagram is of human chromosomes. In humans, each cell normally contains 23 pairs of chromosomes, or a total of 46. 22 of these pairs, called autosomes, look the same in both males and females. The 23rd pair, the sex chromosomes, varies between males and females.

subject: Science
q: How many chromosomes are present in a human cell?
skill: Define heredity and recognise its importance in transferring characteristics
feedback: Humans inherit 23 chromosomes from the father and 23 from the mother. Thus, they have a total of 46 chromosomes or 23 pairs of chromosomes.

subject: Science
q: Chromosomes carry _____ that control all characteristics.
skill: Define heredity and recognise its importance in transferring characteristics
feedback: Chromosomes are threadlike structures of DNA and proteins found in the nucleus of most living cells. They carry genetic information in the form of genes. Genes are segments of the DNA that code for specific characteristics.

subject: Science
q: Inheritable traits that get passed down from one generation to the next include
options: eye colour, greeting customs, and parenting skills.
skin colour, attached earlobes, and table manners.
free earlobes, attached earlobes, and table manners.
hair colour, eye colour, and skin colour.
skill: Define heredity and recognise its importance in transferring characteristics
feedback:
An inherited trait is a feature or characteristic of an organism that has been passed on to the organism in its genes. In humans, the inheritable traits include hair colour, eye colour, skin colour, dimples, tongue rolling and many others.
Greeting customs, parenting skills, and table manners are characteristics learned in our lifetime.

subject: Science
q: The nucleus contains thread-like structures called _____.
skill: Define heredity and recognise its importance in transferring characteristics
feedback: Chromosomes are threadlike structures made up of DNA and proteins found in the nucleus of most living cells. Chromosomes carry genetic information in the form of genes.

subject: Science
q: _____ is the study of how living things pass traits or characteristics from one generation to another.
skill: Define heredity and recognise its importance in transferring characteristics
feedback: Genetics is the study of heredity. Heredity is a biological process where a parent passes certain genes onto their children or offspring.

subject: Science
q: Which structure inside the cell is the basis of heredity?
skill: Define heredity and recognise its importance in transferring characteristics
feedback: Inside the nucleus there are small units called chromosomes which are the basis of heredity information. Every human cell contains 46 chromosomes or 23 pairs of chromosomes. The chromosomes contain important proteins and the hereditary chemical material called DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid).

subject: Science
q: The transfer of characteristics from parents to offspring is
skill: Define heredity and recognise its importance in transferring characteristics
feedback: Heredity is the passing of traits from parents to their offspring.

subject: Science
q: ______ is a constantly occurring process in the human body.
skill: Describe the process and importance of mitosis in an animal cell
feedback: Mitosis is always occurring in a body. Living beings replace old cells with new and healthy ones through mitosis.

subject: Science
q: The final act of dividing in mitosis is called ____.
skill: Describe the process and importance of mitosis in an animal cell
feedback: The division of the cell at the end of mitosis is made possible by cytokinesis. Cytokinesis is the division of cytoplasm. The cytoplasm is pinched in half to create two identical daughter cells.

subject: Science
q: _____ are two tiny structures in an animal cell, located in the cytoplasm near the nuclear membrane.
skill: Describe the process and importance of mitosis in an animal cell
feedback: Centrioles are located near the nucleus in an animal cell. Centrioles move towards the poles of the cell during cell division. As the cell undergoes cell division, the spindle fibres emerge out from the centrioles.

subject: Science
q: Each chromosome pair divides into a pair of identical chromatids jointed one another by the ______.
skill: Describe the process and importance of mitosis in an animal cell
feedback: Chromatids are identical and are joined to one another by the centromere in the middle.

subject: Science
q: Chromosomes condense and become visible during the _____.
skill: Describe the process and importance of mitosis in an animal cell
feedback: Chromosomes are evenly spread out in a cell. However, when mitosis occurs the chromosomes condense at point and become visible in the prophase.

subject: Science
q: Unlike mitosis, the number of chromosomes is halved during _____.
skill: Describe the process and importance of mitosis in an animal cell
feedback: Reproductive cells in animals divide by the process of meiosis rather mitosis. The four daughter cells produced as the end of meiosis carry half the number of chromosomes as that of a parent cell.

subject: Science
q: The healing process in animals is done by _____.
skill: Describe the process and importance of mitosis in an animal cell
feedback: Mitosis creates two identical copies from a parent cell. Healing and replacing of skin and organs are driven by this replication. The skin cells surrounding the site of wound undergo mitosis to make more copies of themselves and thus, the wound heals.

subject: Science
q: Our characteristics are determined by the _____.
skill: Describe the process and importance of meiosis in an animal cell
feedback: Individual characteristics are determined by crossing-over during meiosis-I. While crossing-over, the sister chromatids exchange chromosomal material. This process results in a variety of genetic combinations that lead to variation among individuals of a same family.

subject: Science
q: Sister chromatids are attached at the centromere and align at the centre of the cell with the help of a spindle fibre. How are the sister chromatids separated from each other?
options: Spindle fibres shorten and move the chromatids towards the poles.
Spindle fibres elongate and move towards the centre of the cell.
skill: Describe the process and importance of meiosis in an animal cell
feedback: In anaphase-II, the sister chromatids are pulled apart as the spindle fibres shorten. Additionally, the ends of the cells are pulled further apart.

subject: Science
q: A cell has undergone meiosis-II, but it has not split into four. What will be the next step in the process of cell division to split the cell further?
skill: Describe the process and importance of meiosis in an animal cell
feedback: Cytokinesis is the division of cytoplasm. A cell that undergoes meiosis II, it divides into four cells with half the number of chromosomes as the parent cell.

subject: Science
q: What are the pairs of chromosomes called?
skill: Describe the process and importance of meiosis in an animal cell
feedback: Chromosomes that are similar are called homologous chromosomes.

subject: Science
q: Meiosis does not include _____.
options: production of cells with an equal number of chromosomes as the original cell
production of cells with half the number of chromosomes as the parent cells
skill: Describe the process and importance of meiosis in an animal cell
feedback: Meiosis produces four cells at the end of all the phases. These cells are called gametes. Gametes have half the number of chromosomes that the parent cell has.

subject: Science
q: Which stage of meiosis is shown in the image?
skill: Describe the process and importance of meiosis in an animal cell
feedback: During metaphase-II, the chromosomes align randomly at the equator of the cell. At this stage, the sister chromatids are no longer identical.

subject: Science
q: A cell with a chromosome number of 24 undergoes meiosis, how many chromosomes will there be in each daughter cell?
skill: Describe the process and importance of meiosis in an animal cell
feedback: Meiosis produces <u>half the number of chromosomes</u> as the parent cell. Therefore, if the parent cell with 24 chromosomes undergoes meiosis, the daughter cells will have 12 chromosomes each.

subject: Science
q: How does the variation among siblings occur?
options: Because of the crossing-over during meiosis-I.
Because of the replication of chromosomes.
skill: Describe the process and importance of meiosis in an animal cell
feedback: The sister chromatids exchange their chromosomal material during prophase-I. This process is called crossing over. The chromosomes randomly arrange themselves at the equator of the cell during metaphase-I and move towards the opposite poles afterwards to divide the cell.

subject: Science
q: Receptors and effectors work together as part of a bigger system called the _____.
skill: Define sensitivity, and the terms associated with the nervous system
feedback: The nervous system is responsible for controlling our bodily functions. It consists of many receptors and effectors working together.

subject: Science
q: Only a system that is very sensitive will react to a ______ stimulus.
skill: Define sensitivity, and the terms associated with the nervous system
feedback: Sensitivity is a measure of how strong a signal needs to be to respond. Therefore, only a very sensitive system will respond to a small stimulus.

subject: Science
q: Sarah bumped into Abdullah, and he immediately started crying. Why?
options: He has a lot of nerve cells.
Abdullah's nervous system was not working.
Abdullah has a very sensitive nervous system.
skill: Define sensitivity, and the terms associated with the nervous system
feedback: Sensitivity is a measure of how strong a signal needs to be to generate a reaction. Therefore, we can say Abdullah has a very sensitive nervous system because of his reaction.

subject: Science
q: Plants use photosynthesis to grow. This process requires _____ as a stimulus.
skill: Define sensitivity, and the terms associated with the nervous system
feedback: Photosynthesis is a process in which plants use light energy from the sun to grow. Cells present in plant leaves are stimulated by light.

subject: Science
q: What is responsible for keeping our heart and other vital organs functioning while we sleep?
skill: Define sensitivity, and the terms associated with the nervous system
feedback: We can survive for a short time period without some systems functioning properly. However, other systems, like the nervous system, are critical even while we are sleeping. The nervous system is responsible for generating all our conscious and unconscious activities, and for ensuring all other systems are running.

subject: Science
q: The more _____ your fingers are, the more easily they can react to heat.
skill: Define sensitivity, and the terms associated with the nervous system
feedback: Sensitivity is measure of how strong a signal needs to be to generate a reaction. If your fingers are very sensitive, they will feel heat more easily.

subject: Science
q: _____ are an example of effectors.
skill: Define sensitivity, and the terms associated with the nervous system
feedback: Glands release different chemicals in the body as a response to the signals received by the body.

subject: Science
q: All our five sense organs are _____.
skill: Define sensitivity, and the terms associated with the nervous system
feedback: Organs that are able to receive a stimulus are called receptors. The organs that are involved in reacting are called effectors.

subject: Science
q:
The diagram shows the link between different types of neurons.



What is the direction of the flow of a nerve impulse?
skill: Describe the nervous system, and the structure and function of neurons
feedback: The nerve impulse flows from the sensory neuron (I) to the interneuron (II). The impulse then travels to the motor neuron (III).

subject: Science
q: The diagram shows a type of neuron. What does this neuron do?
options: This is a motor neuron. It carries nerve impulses from the central nervous system to the effectors.
This is a sensory neuron. It carries nerve impulses from sense organs to the central nervous system.
This is an interneuron. It forms a link between different types of neurons.
This is a bacterium. It harms the spinal cord.
skill: Describe the nervous system, and the structure and function of neurons
feedback: This is a motor neuron. Motor neurons carry nerve impulses from the central nervous system to the effectors (the muscles or glands). A motor neuron has a cell body, many dendrites, and a single, long axon.

subject: Science
q: The diagram below shows a type of neuron. What neuron is this?
options: It's a motor neuron. It carries nerve impulses from the central nervous system to effectors.
It's a sensory neuron. It carries nerve impulses from the sense organs to the central nervous system.
It's an interneuron. It forms a link between different types of neurons.
It's a bacterium. It helps in the digestion of food.
skill: Describe the nervous system, and the structure and function of neurons
feedback: Sensory neurons are nerve cells within the nervous system responsible for carrying nerve impulses from sense organs to the central nervous system. A sensory neuron has a circular cell body, long dendrites, and a short axon.

subject: Science
q: _____ neurons within the nervous system are responsible for converting external stimuli from the organism's environment into internal impulses.
skill: Describe the nervous system, and the structure and function of neurons
feedback: The sensory neurons are responsible for converting external stimuli from the organism's environment into internal impulses. They carry impulses (messages) from the sense organs like the eye, nose, or skin to the central nervous system.

subject: Science
q: What is the correct order for the pathway of a message to the effector organs?
options: central nervous system &#8594 nerve impulse &#8594message processing  &#8594motor neuron &#8594 effectors
central nervous system &#8594 message processing &#8594 nerve impulse &#8594sensory neuron &#8594 effectors
central nervous system &#8594 message processing &#8594 nerve impulse &#8594motor neurons &#8594 effectors
central nervous system &#8594 nerve impulse &#8594message processing  &#8594inter neuron &#8594 effectors
skill: Describe the nervous system, and the structure and function of neurons
feedback: A stimulus is detected by special sensors called receptors. An impulse is generated as a result and is carried along the sensory neuron to reach the central nervous system. In the central nervous system, the message is processed, and instructions in the form of impulses are carried along the motor neuron to the effectors.

subject: Science
q: Motor neurons always carry nerve impulses to the _____ or glands, which generate a response.
skill: Describe the nervous system, and the structure and function of neurons
feedback: Motor neurons carry impulses from the central nervous system to the muscles or glands (effectors), which produce a response.

subject: Science
q: What is the correct order for the pathway of a message to the central nervous system?
options: receptor &#8594 stimulus &#8594 impulse &#8594 motor neuron &#8594 central nervous system
impulse &#8594 receptor &#8594stimulus &#8594 sensory neurons &#8594 central nervous system
effector &#8594 stimulus &#8594impulse &#8594 sensory neurons &#8594 central nervous system
stimulus &#8594 receptor &#8594impulse &#8594 sensory neuron &#8594 central nervous system
skill: Describe the nervous system, and the structure and function of neurons
feedback: A stimulus is detected by special sensors called receptors. The impulse is picked up by the sensory neuron and transmitted to the central nervous system.

subject: Science
q: Scientists who want to study how the human body keeps its balance would take a closer look at the ______.
skill: Describe the structure and functions of the central nervous system
feedback: The cerebellum is the second-largest part of the brain. It carries signals from the spinal cord to different parts of the brain. It is responsible for most muscle movements and for maintaining balance.

subject: Science
q: Akbar has trouble sleeping. It is likely that the part of his brain affected is the _____.
skill: Describe the structure and functions of the central nervous system
feedback: The pons is a structural part of the brain stem responsible for controlling functions like sleep, swallowing, and taste. If there is any damage to the pons, a person may experience sleep disturbances, and at worst, enter a comatose state.

subject: Science
q: The nervous system is divided into _____ parts.
skill: Describe the structure and functions of the central nervous system
feedback: The nervous system is divided into two parts: the central nervous system and the peripheral nervous system.

subject: Science
q: The forebrain consists of the _____.
skill: Describe the structure and functions of the central nervous system
feedback: The forebrain consists of three parts, namely the cerebrum, thalamus and hypothalamus. Along with the forebrain, the brain also consists of the hindbrain and the midbrain.

subject: Science
q: Pain is registered in the brain by the stimulation of _____.
skill: Describe the structure and functions of the central nervous system
feedback: Pain is the stimulation of nerve endings present throughout the body. Nerves consist of bundles of fibres that transmit impulses between the brain and all the other parts of the body.

subject: Science
q: The brain and spinal cord are the control centre of the nervous system. Damage to the spinal cord often results in _____.
skill: Describe the structure and functions of the central nervous system
feedback: Motor skills are controlled by the hindbrain, which distributes nerves through the spinal cord. Damage to the spinal cord affects motor skills and often results in the paralysis of different parts of the body.

subject: Science
q: The hindbrain consists of the _____, pons and medulla oblongata.
skill: Describe the structure and functions of the central nervous system
feedback: The cerebellum is the second largest part of the brain and is responsible for muscle movements, balance and posture.

subject: Science
q: The thalamus processes all senses except ____.
skill: Describe the structure and functions of the central nervous system
feedback: The thalamus processes and transmits information from all senses except smell. The thalamus is the largest part of the diencephalon.

subject: Science
q: The small structure in the cerebrum called the _____ makes up the core of the brain.
skill: Describe the structure and functions of the central nervous system
feedback: The diencephalon is the core of the brain and consists of the thalamus and the hypothalamus.

subject: Science
q: The _____ makes up over three quarters of the brain's volume.
skill: Describe the structure and functions of the central nervous system
feedback: The cerebrum is the largest portion of the brain and is responsible for higher functions like interpreting touch, vision and hearing, as well as speech, reasoning, emotions, learning, and the fine control of movement.

subject: Science
q: The largest section of the brain is called the _____.
skill: Describe the structure and functions of the central nervous system
feedback: The forebrain is the biggest section of the human brain. It consists of three main parts: the cerebrum, the thalamus, and the hypothalamus.

subject: Science
q: The human brain is enclosed in a bone known as the _____.
skill: Describe the structure and functions of the central nervous system
feedback: The human brain is very delicate. To protect it from damage, it is surrounded by a hard bone called the cranium.

subject: Science
q: _____ are a bundle of fibres that convey signals to and from the brain.
skill: Describe the structure and functions of the central nervous system
feedback: Nerves are a bundle of fibres that convey signals to and from the brain. These signals are sent to the brain where they are analysed, and a signal is sent back through the nerves to determine the appropriate action.

subject: Science
q: The nervous system can be divided into two parts: the peripheral and the _____ nervous system.
skill: Describe the structure and functions of the central nervous system
feedback: The nervous system consists of the peripheral nervous system and the central nervous system. The central nervous system can be further divided into the brain and the spinal cord.

subject: Science
q: The _____ controls your movement, emotions and other bodily functions.
skill: Describe the structure and functions of the central nervous system
feedback: The nervous system consists of the brain, the spinal cord, and all the nerves in the body. This system is responsible for movement, emotions, and other bodily functions.

subject: Science
q: Reflexes are _____ actions.
skill: Describe the structure and functions of the peripheral nervous system
feedback: The knee-jerk reaction is a reflex. That means our body carries out the action before our brain can knowingly process it. Such actions are involuntary, like pulling our hand away from a hot stove. All reflexes are involuntary actions, but not all involuntary actions are reflexes.

subject: Science
q: Our heartbeat is a(n) _____ action controlled by the autonomic nervous system.
skill: Describe the structure and functions of the peripheral nervous system
feedback: We cannot control our heartbeat. It is also not a reaction or reflex to an outside stimulus. It is an involuntary action controlled by our body's autonomic nervous system.

subject: Science
q: The peripheral nervous system is divided into _____ parts.
skill: Describe the structure and functions of the peripheral nervous system
feedback: The peripheral nervous system can be divided into two parts: the somatic nervous system and the autonomic nervous system

subject: Science
q: The peripheral system allows the brain and spinal cord to receive and send information to other areas of the body, which _____.
options: allows us to think and dream
allows us to react to stimuli in our environment
only allows us to see, smell and hear things in the environment
only allows us to taste and feel things in the environment
skill: Describe the structure and functions of the peripheral nervous system
feedback: The peripheral nervous system allows the brain and spinal cord to receive and send information to other areas of the body. Therefore, it allows us to react to stimuli in our environment picked up by the brain and spinal cord.

subject: Science
q: The somatic nervous system and the autonomic nervous system make up the _____.
skill: Describe the structure and functions of the peripheral nervous system
feedback: The somatic nervous system and the autonomic nervous system make up our peripheral nervous system. The central nervous system consists of the brain and spinal cord.

subject: Science
q: Pain is registered in the brain by the stimulation of _____.
skill: Describe the structure and functions of the peripheral nervous system
feedback: Pain is registered through the stimulation of nerve cells or neurons present throughout the body.

subject: Science
q: Nerves impulses are carried in the form of _____, which are interpreted by the brain.
skill: Describe the structure and functions of the peripheral nervous system
feedback: Nerves transmit messages very fast, so we can act and react quickly. This is why they are carried in the form electrochemical changes. When a nerve is stimulated, it releases an electrical pulse that travels to the brain where it is interpreted.

subject: Science
q: The brain and spinal cord are the control centre of the nervous system. Damage to the somatic nervous system often results in _____.
skill: Describe the structure and functions of the peripheral nervous system
feedback: Motor skills are controlled by the hindbrain, which distributes nerves through the spinal cord. Diseases and damage to the somatic nervous system affect motor skills and often results in the paralysis of different parts of the body.

subject: Science
q: The brain controls
skill: Differentiate between voluntary and involuntary actions and describe reflex actions
feedback: The brain processes all your voluntary and involuntary actions. Reflex actions go through your spinal cord to create an immediate response.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following is a reflex action?
skill: Differentiate between voluntary and involuntary actions and describe reflex actions
feedback: The sneeze reflex is a coordinated response to the irritation of the upper respiratory system, especially the nasal opening and passages. Sneezing is a reflex action that does not require conscious control. Your heartbeat is controlled by the brain and is an involuntary action but not a reflex.

subject: Science
q: When a bright beam of light hit a person's eye, the pupil contracts. In normal light, the pupil will expand again. Identify the actions that have taken place.
options: These are involuntary actions that are similar to breathing. They are  processed by the brain, which sends impulses to contract the pupil.
These are reflex actions in which the pupil expands to protect the eye.
These are voluntary actions in which we contract the pupil so less light enters our eye.
These are reflex actions in which the pupil contracts to protect the eye.
skill: Differentiate between voluntary and involuntary actions and describe reflex actions
feedback: Reflexes such as the pupil reflex take place when a bright beam of light hits a person's eye. The pupil automatically contracts or becomes smaller in order to prevent damage to our eye in bright light.

subject: Science
q: Identify the labels in the given diagram.
options: I is a motor neuron, II is a sensory neuron, and III is an interneuron.
I is an interneuron, II is a sensory neuron, and III is a motor neuron
I is a sensory neuron, II is an interneuron, and III is a motor neuron
I is a motor neuron, II is an interneuron neuron, and III is a sensory neuron
skill: Differentiate between voluntary and involuntary actions and describe reflex actions
feedback: I is a sensory neuron carrying impulses from the sense receptors in the skin. II is an interneuron connecting the sensory neuron to the motor neuron, which is III. This takes impulses to the effectors.

subject: Science
q: Which of these is an involuntary action?
skill: Differentiate between voluntary and involuntary actions and describe reflex actions
feedback: Although we can control our breathing, it mostly happens automatically, making it an involuntary action. Running, sleeping, and eating are all activities we consciously decide to do.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following can be both a voluntary and an involuntary action?
skill: Differentiate between voluntary and involuntary actions and describe reflex actions
feedback: Blinking can be done voluntarily by choosing to close your eyes and open them. However, it is also involuntary since we blink around 28,800 times a day. You cannot however control the digestion of food in your body, your heartbeat, or the growth of your hair.

subject: Science
q: The spinal cord transmits nerve impulses from the
options: sensory neurons to the brain, and back from the brain to the motor neurons.
motor neurons to the brain, and back from the brain to the sensory neurons.
interneurons to the brain, and back from the brain to the sensory neurons.
sensory neurons to the brain, and back from the brain to the sensory neurons.
skill: Differentiate between voluntary and involuntary actions and describe reflex actions
feedback: The spinal cord transmits nerve impulses from sensory neurons to the brain. The brain processes it and then sends instructions through motor neurons to the effectors.

subject: Science
q: The biological process in which ammonia is reduced to nitrites by bacteria is called
skill: Explain the nitrogen cycle
feedback: The biological process in which ammonium gets converted into nitrites and then nitrates is known as nitrification. Further, when these nitrates are converted or reduced into nitrogen gas, it is called denitrification. These steps involve various microorganisms, and it is important biologically as well as economically.

subject: Science
q: Which form of nitrogen is taken by the producers of the ecosystem?
skill: Explain the nitrogen cycle
feedback: Our atmosphere is a reservoir of free gaseous nitrogen. Producers of the ecosystem cannot pick up the gaseous nitrogen directly from atmosphere. It has to be converted into nitrates to be utilised by producers.

subject: Science
q: Plants form _____ as a result of assimilation.
skill: Explain the nitrogen cycle
feedback: Assimilation refers to the utilisation of nitrates by organisms. The nitrates absorbed by plants are used for making proteins. Proteins play an important role in the growth and survival of plants and animals. Nitrogen is an important component of protein.

subject: Science
q: _____ is the conversion of nitrogen gas into nitrates.
skill: Explain the nitrogen cycle
feedback: The first step of the nitrogen cycle is nitrogen fixation. It refers to the conversion of nitrogen gas into nitrates and can occur via three possible ways, namely atmospheric, biological, or industrial.

subject: Science
q: Denitrifying bacteria reduce nitrates and nitrites to nitrogen gas by a process called _____.
skill: Explain the nitrogen cycle
feedback: The final step of the nitrogen cycle is denitrification. It refers to the conversion of nitrates and nitrites into nitrogen gas carried out by denitrifying bacteria.

subject: Science
q: Nitrogen is an important component of which biomolecule?
skill: Explain the nitrogen cycle
feedback: Nitrogen is an important component of proteins and nucleic acids. Fats, lipids, and carbohydrates don't contain nitrogen.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following is responsible for breaking down proteins of dead organisms and nitrogenous waste?
skill: Explain the nitrogen cycle
feedback: Ammonification is the breaking down of proteins of dead organisms and nitrogenous waste into ammonia. This process is carried out by ammonifying bacteria.

subject: Science
q: Some pesticides have been found to negatively affect nitrification. Which of the following would be hindered as a result?
options: conversion of proteins of dead organisms into ammonia
conversion of nitrogen gas into nitrates
conversion of ammonia into nitrites and nitrates
conversion of nitrates into nitrogen gas
skill: Explain the nitrogen cycle
feedback: Nitrification refers to the process where nitrifying bacteria converts ammonia into nitrates and nitrites. If any pesticide affects nitrification, the bacteria would not be able to convert ammonia into nitrites and nitrates.

subject: Science
q: Which trophic level has organisms that are responsible for releasing carbon dioxide back into the environment?
skill: Explain the carbon cycle
feedback: All living beings, whether they are producers, consumers, or decomposers release carbon dioxide back into the environment. Organisms in every trophic level respire, therefore they exhale carbon dioxide into the environment. Decomposers release carbon dioxide by breaking down organic compounds of dead plants and animals.

subject: Science
q: Which trophic level has organisms that are responsible for converting carbon dioxide into complex organic compounds?
skill: Explain the carbon cycle
feedback: Producers take carbon dioxide from the environment and convert it into complex organic compounds via photosynthesis. Only producers have the ability to do this. Organisms in every other trophic level rely on plants for carbon dioxide fixation.

subject: Science
q: At what step of the given carbon cycle have there been changes that resulted in global warming?
skill: Explain the carbon cycle
feedback: For 200 years, human beings have been using fossil fuels to meet their energy requirements. However, as a result, more carbon dioxide has been released into environment. The combustion of fossil fuels has led to higher concentrations of carbon dioxide in the environment. Since carbon dioxide is a greenhouse gas, it has played a major role in increasing the temperature of the surface of the Earth.

subject: Science
q: A researcher is conducting an ecological study on a small island. She observes that the concentration of carbon dioxide in the air has steadily increased over the last five years. She claims that the amount of carbon on Earth has increased. Do you agree with her claim?
options: Yes, carbon dioxide has increased in the air, therefore there is more carbon on earth.
No, the amount of carbon has decreased as fewer fossil fuels are left.
No, the amount of carbon on Earth is fixed, it only changes from one form to another.
Yes, the amount of carbon has increased because of the burning of fossil fuels.
skill: Explain the carbon cycle
feedback: Although carbon can exist in various forms, the amount of carbon on Earth is constant. Carbon can be present in living things, fossil fuels, or in the air as carbon dioxide. It can change from one form to another, however, the total amount of carbon on Earth stays the same. Only high concentration of carbon dioxide (since it's a greenhouse gas) contributes towards global warming. Carbon, in any other form, doesn't contribute towards global warming.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following processes is responsible for releasing carbon dioxide back to the environment?
skill: Explain the carbon cycle
feedback:
Respiration alone results in the production and release of carbon dioxide.

Photosynthesis releases oxygen back into the environment.
Nitrification refers to the conversion of ammonia into nitrites and nitrates facilitated by nitrifying bacteria.
The absorption of nitrates and nitrites by plants in called assimilation.

subject: Science
q: Whaling is the hunting of whales to make usable products such as meat and oil. Some scientists claim that whaling has contributed towards global warming. Do you agree?
options: No, as whaling would affect ocean life only and not contribute towards global warming.
Yes, as whales are hunted to produce oil, and the burning of oil releases methane.
Yes, as whaling leads to the release of extra carbon dioxide in the air.
No, as the carbon dioxide released by whaling and the production of usable products, is absorbed by producers again.
skill: Explain the carbon cycle
feedback:
In whales, carbon is present in the form of complex organic compounds. However, as a result of whaling and the production of usable products, that carbon gets released into the atmosphere in the form of carbon dioxide. Since carbon dioxide is a greenhouse gas, higher concentrations of it contribute towards global warming.

The population of whales in the oceans has decreased due to whaling. All the carbon that was once in the bodies of whales is now in the atmosphere in the form of carbon dioxide. Therefore, whaling has contributed towards global warming.

subject: Science
q: Without _____, carbon would be trapped in dead organisms.
skill: Explain the carbon cycle
feedback: Decomposers feed on dead organisms by breaking down complex organic compounds into energy and carbon dioxide. The energy released is used by decomposers to carry out cellular activities. The carbon dioxide produced is released back into the environment. Without decomposers, carbon would remain stuck in the form of complex organic compounds in dead organisms.

subject: Science
q: At night, most of the sun's energy
options: helps in the growth of plants.
skill: Explain the greenhouse effect and global warming
feedback: Earth's atmosphere does the same thing as the greenhouse. Gases in the atmosphere such as carbon dioxide do what the roof of a greenhouse does. During the day, the Sun shines through the atmosphere. Earth's surface warms up due to the sunlight. At night, Earth's surface cools down and releases the heat into the air. However, some of the heat is trapped by the greenhouse gases in the atmosphere. This is what keeps our Earth warm.

subject: Science
q: A greenhouse is made of _____.
skill: Explain the greenhouse effect and global warming
feedback: A greenhouse is a house made of glass. It has glass walls and a glass roof. Sunlight enters and refracts, thus getting trapped inside. Along with sunlight, heat also enters the greenhouse, thus making it warm inside. People grow tomatoes, flowers, and other plants inside the greenhouse under controlled climatic conditions. A greenhouse stays warm even during winter.

subject: Science
q: _____ will affect agriculture and food production around the world.
skill: Explain the greenhouse effect and global warming
feedback: Climate change will affect agriculture and food production around the world. For example, factors such as projected increases in temperatures, changes in precipitation patterns, extreme weather conditions, and a decrease in the availability of water may all result in reduced agricultural productivity.

subject: Science
q: What is the main reason of the melting of ice sheets?
options: an increase in the amount of oxygen
a decrease in the amount of carbon dioxide
skill: Explain the greenhouse effect and global warming
feedback: Global warming is affecting various parts of the world. It is accelerating the melting of ice sheets, permafrost, and glaciers, thus increasing the average sea levels to a great extent.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following takes place due to global warming?
skill: Explain the greenhouse effect and global warming
feedback: Global warming causes changes in temperature as well as changes in the amount of rainfall. The fluctuations in rainfall lead to various problems such as floods or droughts.

subject: Science
q: Global warming causes sea levels to _____.
skill: Explain the greenhouse effect and global warming
feedback:
Global warming has consequences for our oceans, weather, food, and health.
Ice sheets such as Greenland and Antarctica are melting. This causes sea levels to rise, which, in turn, causes floods in coastal areas.

subject: Science
q: Due to an increase in greenhouse gases, Earth's average temperature is rising, and the planet is getting warmer. This is known as _____.
skill: Explain the greenhouse effect and global warming
feedback: Due to human activities like burning different fuels and industrial processes, the amount of greenhouse gases gradually increases in the atmosphere. As the layer of greenhouse gases around Earth becomes thicker, it traps more heat. This results in increasing the average temperature of the planet, thus, Earth is getting warmer and warmer. This is called global warming.

subject: Science
q: Due to the burning of fuels, the amount of greenhouse gases has _____ in the atmosphere.
skill: Explain the greenhouse effect and global warming
feedback: Due to human activities like burning different fuels and industrial processes, the amount of greenhouse gases is increased in the atmosphere.

subject: Science
q: A human activity that leads directly to the extinction of wildlife is _____.
skill: Describe the effect of humans on wildlife habitats, and deforestation, and energy resources
feedback: The human activity that leads directly to the extinction of wildlife is the destruction of wildlife habitats. Destroying habitats to make room for our housing, recreational activities and industries results in the extinction of various species.

subject: Science
q: Explain the relationship between extinction and biodiversity.
options: Extinction has no impact on biodiversity.
Extinction contributes to a decrease in biodiversity.
Extinction randomly changes biodiversity in one direction or the other.
Extinction contributes to an increase in biodiversity.
skill: Describe the effect of humans on wildlife habitats, and deforestation, and energy resources
feedback: As a result of deforestation and pollution, wildlife habitats are badly affected. Hunting, pollution, and the encroachment of humans on formerly wild habitats have increased the rate of extinction. With fewer animal species surviving, biodiversity goes down.

subject: Science
q: The variety of plant and animal life in the world or in a particular habitat is known as _____.
skill: Describe the effect of humans on wildlife habitats, and deforestation, and energy resources
feedback: Biodiversity is the variety of plant and animal life in the world or in a particular habitat. In general, more biodiversity is an indication of a more stable and healthier environment.

subject: Science
q: One irreversible effect of both deforestation and water pollution on the environment is the _____.
options: decrease in atmospheric carbon dioxide levels
skill: Describe the effect of humans on wildlife habitats, and deforestation, and energy resources
feedback: Irreversible means that you cannot undo it. Once an organism is extinct, it cannot come back. When we cut down trees, we remove habitats and food sources for numerous species that rely on them for survival. Similarly, water pollution is harming animals that rely on water for their survival. Both these factors can result in the extinction of wildlife.

subject: Science
q: Deforestation will most likely result in an increase in _____.
skill: Describe the effect of humans on wildlife habitats, and deforestation, and energy resources
feedback: Deforestation (the cutting of trees) stops trees from removing carbon dioxide from the atmosphere through photosynthesis. So, when we cut down trees, levels of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere will increase. And a higher level of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere contributes to global warming.

subject: Science
q: Consequences of burning fossil fuel include _____.
skill: Describe the effect of humans on wildlife habitats, and deforestation, and energy resources
feedback: Most of the air pollution results from the burning of fossil fuels, such as coal, oil, natural gas, and gasoline which are burnt to produce electricity and power for our homes and vehicles.

subject: Science
q: Fossil fuels _____.
skill: Describe the effect of humans on wildlife habitats, and deforestation, and energy resources
feedback: Fossil fuels are natural resources such as coal, petroleum and natural gas. They take thousands of years to form naturally and cannot be recycled or renewed. At present, the main energy source used by humans is non-renewable fossil fuels.

subject: Science
q:
Consider the food chain below.

grass \[\rightarrow\] rabbit \[\rightarrow\] fox \[\rightarrow\] bear \[\rightarrow\] mushroom

How many types of decomposers are present?
skill: Describe levels of ecological organisation
feedback:
In the above food chain, only mushrooms are decomposers.

Organic matter is recycled in an ecosystem by decomposers. Decomposers are organisms such as bacteria and fungi that break down the organic matter in the dead bodies of plants and animals. As the decomposers feed from the dead animals, they break down the organic compounds into simple nutrients.

subject: Science
q: The study of the interrelationship between organisms and their environment is called _____
skill: Describe levels of ecological organisation
feedback: Ecology is the branch of biology which studies the interactions among organisms and their environment. Objects of study include interactions of organisms with each other and with the abiotic components of their environment.

subject: Science
q: Organisms in the ecosystem that are responsible for the recycling of plant and animal wastes are
skill: Describe levels of ecological organisation
feedback: The group of organisms called decomposers forms the final link in the food chain. They break down dead animals and plants and return vital nutrients to the soil. Some decomposers, like fungi, can be seen without a microscope, but much of the decomposition process is carried out by microscopic bacteria.

subject: Science
q:
Fill in the blank below for a typical food chain.

producer \[\rightarrow\] primary consumer \[\rightarrow\] secondary consumer \[\rightarrow\]_____
skill: Describe levels of ecological organisation
feedback: A food chain represents a series of events in which food and energy are transferred from one organism in an ecosystem to another. Food chains show how energy is passed from the sun to producers, from producers to primary consumers, and eventually to tertiary consumers.

subject: Science
q: A group of organisms of the same species living together in a habitat is called a/an _____.
skill: Describe levels of ecological organisation
feedback:
Population is defined as a group of organisms of the same species living together in the same habitat.

A community is defined as multiple populations living in the same habitat.

An ecosystem is defined as a collection of all the biotic (living) and abiotic (non-living) factors of the environment.

A niche is the role that any given organism plays in an ecosystem.

subject: Science
q: A group of organisms belonging to different species living together in a habitat is called a/an _____.
skill: Describe levels of ecological organisation
feedback:
A community is defined as multiple populations living in the same habitat. In simpler terms, it means members of different species living together.

All the ecosystems on Earth are collectively referred to as the biosphere.
An ecosystem is defined as a collection of all the biotic (living) and abiotic (non-living) factors of an environment.

A niche is the role that any given organism plays in an ecosystem.

subject: Science
q: An ocean is an example of a/an _____.
skill: Describe levels of ecological organisation
feedback:
An ecosystem is a collection of all the biotic and abiotic factors of an environment.
An ocean is considered an ecosystem as it contains both biotic (living) and abiotic (non-living) factors which consistently interact with one another as a system.
A community is defined as multiple populations living together in the same habitat.
A niche is the role that any given organism plays in an ecosystem.
All the ecosystems on Earth are collectively referred to as the biosphere.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following is an example of an artificial ecosystem?
skill: Describe levels of ecological organisation
feedback: A man-made ecosystem is called an artificial ecosystem. Among all the options, only a zoo is man-made. All the other options exist naturally.

subject: Science
q: How can a food chain be affected by changes in the Earth's climate?
options: Climate change doesn't affect any food chain or food web.
Tigers and lions would stop hunting in winters and summers.
Climate change can cause extinction of a species in an ecosystem.
All animals would start starving due to climate change.
skill: Explain food webs and food chains
feedback:
Climate changes can affect a food chain in many ways.
It can cause some species in an ecosystem to migrate if they are not adapted to the new climate. It can also cause the extinction of some species, which can disturb the whole food web.

subject: Science
q:
The given image shows the pyramid of biotic components in an ecosystem.


Only a small amount of the energy in an organism is transferred to the next organism in this pyramid. What happens to the remaining energy in a level?
options: The remaining energy is lost in the air.
It is used by the organism for different life processes.
Producers use the remaining energy to make food.
skill: Explain food webs and food chains
feedback: An organism uses its energy to complete different life processes. When another organism feeds on it, only 10% of its energy is transferred to the next organism.

subject: Science
q: A tertiary consumer feeds on secondary consumers. Where does the energy in a tertiary consumer go?
options: Decomposers return energy into the soil after tertiary consumers die.
Tertiary consumers become the food of producers when they get old.
Energy in tertiary consumers remains in their body forever.
Primary consumers absorb the energy of tertiary consumers.
skill: Explain food webs and food chains
feedback: Decomposers are organisms that break down the remains of dead animals and plants and transfer the energy in them to soil or air. So, the energy in tertiary or higher level consumers goes back into the soil after they die.

subject: Science
q: What would happen if all the organisms in a species die?
options: A food web shows more organisms when a species becomes extinct.
When a species becomes extinct, other organisms change their habitat to mountains.
There would be no effect on the food chain
The food chain and food web would be highly affected.
skill: Explain food webs and food chains
feedback: When all organisms of a species die, that species is extinct. When a species becomes extinct, the food web in that ecosystem is highly disturbed. This is because organisms that feed on the extinct species begin competing with each other.

subject: Science
q: What role(s) does the lion play in the given food web?
skill: Explain food webs and food chains
feedback:
In the given food web, the lion is a secondary and tertiary consumer.
When a lion feeds on a herbivore, or an animal that eats plants, it is a secondary consumer.


However, when it feeds on a secondary consumer, or an animal that eats another animal, it becomes a tertiary consumer in that food chain.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following links represent an accurate food chain in an aquatic habitat?
skill: Explain food webs and food chains
feedback:
The correct food chain within an aquatic habitat is isalgae → krill → seal → whale.
Algae are organisms in water that make their own food using photosynthesis.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following statements about energy in a food web is TRUE?
options: Secondary consumers get energy from producers indirectly.
In a food chain, energy from a tertiary consumer transfers to a primary consumer.
All the energy in a food web comes from nuclear energy.
Decomposers destroy all the energy in a food chain.
skill: Explain food webs and food chains
feedback: All consumers get their energy from producers directly or indirectly. Primary consumers get their energy from producers directly, and they become a link between producers and other consumers.

subject: Science
q: Introducing a new species of plants from one habitat to another is an example of _____.
skill: Discuss the factors that cause changes in a habitat
feedback: When we introduce a new species of plants from one habitat to another then we might see significant changes in the habitats. This is an example of human impact. Other examples of human impact include deforestation and pollution.

subject: Science
q: Human activities that cause damage to environment and habitats include _____ and _____.
skill: Discuss the factors that cause changes in a habitat
feedback: Humans play a huge role in affecting habitats. Some activities that cause damage to the environment include over-population, overconsumption, pollution, deforestation, or even the introduction of new species.

subject: Science
q: Severe or high temperatures can cause _____.
skill: Discuss the factors that cause changes in a habitat
feedback:
Too much heat can change a habitat. For example, a drought occurs when there is no rainfall for a while.
They usually occur in areas that have high temperatures.

subject: Science
q: All life on earth depends on _____ for food.
skill: Discuss the factors that cause changes in a habitat
feedback: All life on earth depends on plants for food, directly or indirectly. For instance, zebras feed on grass and lions feed on zebras. Humans also get their food from plants, such as vegetables, fruits, rice and wheat. If there are no or less plants, organisms will suffer due to a shortage of food.

subject: Science
q: Birds travel large distances to find warmer climate and food. This is known as _____.
skill: Discuss the factors that cause changes in a habitat
feedback: Many animals make seasonal movements, or migration. Examples includes birds flying south for the winter and the great wilder beast migration across Africa.

subject: Science
q: Habitats can change due to natural disasters like earthquake, floods, or _____.
skill: Discuss the factors that cause changes in a habitat
feedback: Habitats change all the time. Some of these are changes due to natural disasters like earthquakes, floods, or droughts.

subject: Science
q: Soil, water, air, and light are _____ components.
skill: Discuss the factors that cause changes in a habitat
feedback: Abiotic factors are non-living factors in an ecosystem. As part of the ecosystem, these factors affect the living things in it, but they are not living themselves. For example, soil, water, air, and light are all abiotic factors.

subject: Science
q: Which is a biotic factor that affects the size of a population in a specific habitat?
skill: Discuss the factors that cause changes in a habitat
feedback: Biotic factors are any living component that affects the environment. If the number of predators in an ecosystem increases, the population of a specific species in that habitat decreases. For example, if the number of lions and tigers increase in a savannah, the number of zebras would decrease.

subject: Science
q: Air, water, and soil are _____.
skill: Identify ways of conserving the Earth's environment
feedback: The earth is the only planet where life is possible. Our survival depends on natural resources like air, water, and soil.

subject: Science
q: Burning waste material at a high temperature to produce gas or oil is called _____.
skill: Identify ways of conserving the Earth's environment
feedback: Incineration is a method by which waste is burned at extremely high temperatures to produce gas or oil.

subject: Science
q: _____ is the method of burying solid waste.
skill: Identify ways of conserving the Earth's environment
feedback: Landfill is a method of disposing trash and garbage by burying it between layers of earth.

subject: Science
q: Solid waste should be managed properly by landfilling, _____ or recycling.
skill: Identify ways of conserving the Earth's environment
feedback: Solid waste pollutes the Earth's environment when dumped in open spaces or burnt. Hence, we should not dump it in open places or burn it. Solid waste should be managed properly by landfill, incineration, or recycling.

subject: Science
q: When solid waste is _____, it produces harmful emissions.
skill: Identify ways of conserving the Earth's environment
feedback: When the solid waste is burnt, it produces harmful emissions into the air.

subject: Science
q: Recycling can help lower _____.
options: the rate of extinction of animals
skill: Identify ways of conserving the Earth's environment
feedback: By recycling we can make new things from used items and we can also reduce waste disposal and pollution. Therefore, it is an alternative to waste disposal, which saves material and helps lower greenhouse gas emissions.

subject: Science
q: _____ is the process of converting waste materials into new materials and objects.
skill: Identify ways of conserving the Earth's environment
feedback: Recycling is the process of converting waste materials into new materials and objects. Recycling can prevent the waste of potentially useful materials and reduce the consumption of new raw materials, thereby reducing: energy usage, air pollution (from incineration), and water pollution (from landfilling).

subject: Science
q: The following image shows a/an _____.
skill: Discuss the relationships between organisms with examples
feedback: The given image shows a mosquito. Mosquitoes are parasites for human beings as they don't kill us, but they cause harm to us. Mosquitoes feed on our blood while staying on the outer surface of our bodies, so they are ectoparasites.

subject: Science
q:
Parasites and predators both feed on other organisms. How can we differentiate between a parasite and a predator?

I.   Parasites are usually smaller in size than their hosts.
II.  Predators are always carnivores.
III. Parasites do not kill their hosts at once, but predators kill their prey when they feed on them.
skill: Discuss the relationships between organisms with examples
feedback:
Parasites and predators both feed on other organisms but there are a few differences between them.

Parasites usually do not kill their hosts instantly, but they harm them. For example, weeds affect the growth of crops, but they do not kill the crops. On the other hand, predators kill their prey when they feed on them. For example, a frog kills a fly when it preys on the fly.

Parasites such as lice and mosquitoes are usually smaller in size than their host.
Predators are not always carnivores. They can be herbivores, omnivores, and reptiles as well.

subject: Science
q: Ayesha's head feels itchy. Her mother finds some insects in her head. The insects found could be
skill: Discuss the relationships between organisms with examples
feedback:
Lice are ectoparasites that are usually found externally on mammals and birds.
They are parasites because they feed on their host's blood.

Dandruff is a condition in which one's scalp or skin becomes dry. It can also cause itching but it's not a parasite as it doesn't feed on our blood or skin.

subject: Science
q: The relationship between a frog and mosquitoes is an example of
skill: Discuss the relationships between organisms with examples
feedback: A frog feeds on insects like mosquitoes and flies. As a result, mosquitoes are instantly killed by the frog. This is an example of predation.

subject: Science
q:
Which of the following relationships are examples of mutualism?

I.   humans and mosquitoes
II.  bees and flowers
III. cows and cattle egrets
skill: Discuss the relationships between organisms with examples
feedback:
The relationships between bees and flowers and cows and cattle egrets are examples of mutualism, as both organisms in each relationship benefit each other.
The relationship between a human and mosquitoes is parasitism, as a mosquito harms a human by feeding on its blood and spreading diseases like malaria and dengue.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following statements about relationships in organisms is FALSE?
options: Interaction between organisms can only be in the form of predation and parasitism.
Some organisms can have a relationship of shared benefit with other organisms.
Some organisms can be both parasites and prey in an ecosystem.
Some organisms in an ecosystem can be both prey and predator.
skill: Discuss the relationships between organisms with examples
feedback:
Interaction between organisms can be in many ways. It can be through predation, parasitism, and mutualism.

A friendly relationship in which organisms benefit each other is called mutualism.
Some organisms in an ecosystem can be both parasites and prey, and others can be both prey and predator. For example, a mosquito is a parasite for humans, but it is prey for lizards and frogs. Similarly, a frog is a predator for flies and mosquitoes, but it is prey for snakes.

subject: Science
q: What is common to predators and parasites?
options: Both are larger than their prey.
Both break down the remains of animals and plants.
Both are present inside their host's body.
skill: Discuss the relationships between organisms with examples
feedback:
Predators and parasites both feed on other organisms.
Predators are usually larger in size than their prey, but parasites are usually smaller than their host.
Predators kill their prey to get food, but parasites do not kill their hosts at the time of obtaining food.
Some parasites are present inside the host's body, while other are present on its surfaces.

subject: Science
q: What would happen if all the organisms in a species die?
options: A food web shows more organisms when a species becomes extinct.
When a species becomes extinct, other organisms change their habitat to mountains.
There would be no effect on the food chain
The food chain and food web would be highly affected.
skill: Construct and describe food chains and webs, and flow of energy
feedback: When all organisms of a species die, that species is extinct. When a species becomes extinct, the food web in that ecosystem is highly disturbed. This is because organisms that feed on the extinct species begin competing with each other.

subject: Science
q: A tertiary consumer feeds on secondary consumers. Where does the energy in a tertiary consumer go?
options: Decomposers return energy into the soil after tertiary consumers die.
Tertiary consumers become the food of producers when they get old.
Energy in tertiary consumers remains in their body forever.
Primary consumers absorb the energy of tertiary consumers.
skill: Construct and describe food chains and webs, and flow of energy
feedback: Decomposers are organisms that break down the remains of dead animals and plants and transfer the energy in them to soil or air. So, the energy in tertiary or higher level consumers goes back into the soil after they die.

subject: Science
q:
The given image shows the pyramid of biotic components in an ecosystem.


Only a small amount of the energy in an organism is transferred to the next organism in this pyramid. What happens to the remaining energy in a level?
options: The remaining energy is lost in the air.
It is used by the organism for different life processes.
Producers use the remaining energy to make food.
skill: Construct and describe food chains and webs, and flow of energy
feedback: An organism uses its energy to complete different life processes. When another organism feeds on it, only 10% of its energy is transferred to the next organism.

subject: Science
q: How can a food chain be affected by changes in the Earth's climate?
options: Climate change doesn't affect any food chain or food web.
Tigers and lions would stop hunting in winters and summers.
Climate change can cause extinction of a species in an ecosystem.
All animals would start starving due to climate change.
skill: Construct and describe food chains and webs, and flow of energy
feedback:
Climate changes can affect a food chain in many ways.
It can cause some species in an ecosystem to migrate if they are not adapted to the new climate. It can also cause the extinction of some species, which can disturb the whole food web.

subject: Science
q: When we eat onions, our trophic level is that of a _____.
skill: Construct and describe food chains and webs, and flow of energy
feedback: Primary consumers eat plants directly. The secondary consumer is the organism that eats the primary consumer. So, when we eat onions, our trophic level is that of a primary consumer.

subject: Science
q: In ecosystems, the flow of _____ is one way, while _____ is/are constantly recycled.
skill: Construct and describe food chains and webs, and flow of energy
feedback: An ecosystem survives by a combination of one-way energy flow and minerals that are recycled. Minerals and energy tend to flow in the same direction for most of an ecosystem. The big difference is that the minerals are ultimately recycled in the ecosystem while the energy is ultimately lost from the ecosystem to the universe at large.

subject: Science
q:
Consider the following food chain:

tree \[\rightarrow\] caterpillar \[\rightarrow\] robin \[\rightarrow\] hawk \[\rightarrow\] coyote

Identify the secondary consumer.
skill: Construct and describe food chains and webs, and flow of energy
feedback:
Primary consumers eat plants exclusively and are all herbivores. The secondary consumer is the organism that eats the primary consumer. This animal is normally larger than the primary consumer but smaller than the tertiary consumer.

In above food chain, the tree is a producer, the caterpillar is a primary consumer, the robin is a secondary consumer, and the hawk and coyote are tertiary consumers.

subject: Science
q: The total amount of living or organic matter in an ecosystem at any time is called _____.
skill: Construct and describe food chains and webs, and flow of energy
feedback: The total amount of dry weight of living or organic matter in an ecosystem at any time is called biomass.

subject: Science
q: The special enzyme that unwinds the double helix molecule of DNA is called
skill: Define biotechnology and explain the replication of DNA
feedback: Helicases are a class of enzymes vital to all living organisms. Their main function is to unzip an organism's DNA. Helicase unwinds the DNA and separates and unzips the double-stranded DNA.

subject: Science
q: Which bonds hold together the nitrogenous bases on a DNA strand?
skill: Define biotechnology and explain the replication of DNA
feedback: Strands of DNA are made of the sugar and phosphate groups of the nucleotides, while the middle parts are made of the nitrogenous bases - A, T, G, C. The nitrogenous bases on the two strands of DNA pair up and are held together by weak hydrogen bonds.

subject: Science
q: A strand that has bases ACGAT produces a strand with the complementary bases
skill: Define biotechnology and explain the replication of DNA
feedback: During DNA replication, new nucleotides join to form a new strand. The strand that has bases ACGAT produces a strand with the complementary bases TGCTA. This is in accordance with the base-pairing rule which states that A is always paired with T and G is always paired with C.

subject: Science
q: The image shows a section of nitrogenous bases on one of the old strands - A. After DNA replication, what will be the base sequences of strands B and D?
options: Both will have the sequence ATTGGTC.
Both will have the sequence CGGTTCA.
skill: Define biotechnology and explain the replication of DNA
feedback:
During DNA replication, new bases are added following the rule of base pairing. The rule states that adenine (A) is always paired with thymine (T) and guanine (G) is always paired with (C) cytosine.
The new strand B and the old strand D are both complementary to strand A. Therefore, both B and D will have a base sequence of ATTGGTC complementary to TAACCAG.

subject: Science
q: The replication of DNA takes place in the
skill: Define biotechnology and explain the replication of DNA
feedback: Replication of DNA takes place in the nucleoplasm. Nucleoplasm is a highly viscous liquid contained within the nucleus that surrounds the chromosomes and other sub-nuclear organelles.

subject: Science
q: In the second step of DNA replication, each of the strands produces a new strand using new
skill: Define biotechnology and explain the replication of DNA
feedback:
At the start of DNA replication, the double helix structure is separated into two strands.
Then each of these strands produces a new strand using new nucleotides. In this way, each of the double strands produces its identical copy, and two daughter strands are formed.

subject: Science
q: The diagram shows a process before cell division. Identify the process.
skill: Define biotechnology and explain the replication of DNA
feedback: The process of making identical copies of the DNA double helix using existing DNA as a template for the synthesis of new DNA strands is called DNA replication.

subject: Science
q: What is the sequence of pairing of bases during DNA replication?
skill: Define biotechnology and explain the replication of DNA
feedback:
DNA is made up of nucleotides. Each nucleotide is made up of a sugar group, a phosphate group, and a nitrogenous base.
During DNA replication, new nitrogenous bases are added to form the new strand following the rule of base pairing.
This rule states that adenine (A) is always paired with thymine (T) and guanine (G) is always paired with (C) cytosine.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following is NOT a difference between an animal and bacterial cell?
options: A bacterial cell has a cell wall, while animal cells don't.
A bacterial cell lacks a well-defined nucleus, while animal cells have a well-defined nucleus.
A bacterial cell does not have membrane bound organelles, while an animal cell has membrane bound organelles.
A bacterial cell has organelles such as mitochondria and endoplasmic reticulum, while animal cells do not.
skill: Define bacterium and explain how genes are introduced into a bacterium
feedback: Many of the organelles found inside animal or plant cells such as mitochondria, golgi apparatus, and endoplasmic reticulum are absent in a bacterial cell. The remaining differences are accurate.

subject: Science
q: The microorganism in the given figure is a _____.
skill: Define bacterium and explain how genes are introduced into a bacterium
feedback: This is a diagram of a bacterium. Bacteria, plural for bacterium, are unicellular organisms that can only be seen with the help of a microscope. They are found everywhere like in the air, in water, and in the soil.

subject: Science
q: People with diabetes have low levels of _____.
skill: Define bacterium and explain how genes are introduced into a bacterium
feedback: Diabetes is a disease in which glucose levels in the blood are too high. It is caused by a lack of insulin in the blood.

subject: Science
q: The main function of insulin is to
options: break down glucose so it can be used by our cells.
allow the absorption of nutrients through the small intestine.
break down fat so it can be used by our cells.
break down protein so it can be used by our cells.
skill: Define bacterium and explain how genes are introduced into a bacterium
feedback: Insulin helps the body break down the glucose in the blood and allow it to be used by our cells.

subject: Science
