q: The process in which acids (H<sup>+</sup>) and bases (OH<sup>-</sup>) react to form salt and water is called _____.
skill: Define the terms acid, alkali and salt
feedback: A neutralisation process is when an acid and base react to form water and salt. It involves the combination of H+ ions and OH- ions to generate water.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following household items are alkaline?
skill: Define the terms acid, alkali and salt
feedback: Alkalis are bitter in taste and have a slippery surface. Detergent, toothpaste, and shampoo are alkaline and we use them in our daily lives.

subject: Science
q: All alkalis are bases but not all bases are _____.
skill: Define the terms acid, alkali and salt
feedback: Not all bases are soluble in water. Only those that dissolve in water are alkalis. So, all alkalis are bases but all bases are not alkalis.

subject: Science
q: Sodium hydroxide NaOH, often called "caustic soda", is _____.
options: both a base and an alkali
skill: Define the terms acid, alkali and salt
feedback: Sodium hydroxide NaOH is both a base and an alkali because it's basic in nature and soluble in water.

subject: Science
q: Organic acids are _____ in nature.
skill: Describe the properties and uses of acids
feedback: Organic acids are obtained from natural resources like fruits and vegetables. They have many uses in our daily lives because they are weak. Lactic acid, ascorbic acid, and citric acid are examples of weak or organic acids. However, inorganic acids are usually strong and corrosive. They are obtained from mineral resources. Sulphuric acid is a strong acid and it is an inorganic acid.

subject: Science
q: Hydrochloric acid is neutralised by reacting with _____.
skill: Describe the properties and uses of acids
feedback:
Sodium hydroxide is a base. Acids react with bases to form salt and water. This process is called neutralisation.
acid + base → salt + water
The H+ cation of the acid combines with the OH- anion of the base to form water. The compound formed by the cation of the base and the anion of the acid is called a salt.
Here, sodium hydroxide separates to Na+ and OH- ions. Hydrochloric acid separates in H+ and Cl- ions. H+ and OH- bond with each other to create water (H2O) while Na+ and Cl- bond with each other to form sodium chloride (NaCl).
H+ + Cl- + Na+ + OH- = Na+ + Cl- + H2O
The chemical equation is:
2HCl + 2NaOH → 2NaCl + 2H2O

subject: Science
q: Which gas is given off when zinc reacts with hydrochloric acid?
skill: Describe the properties and uses of acids
feedback:
When an acid reacts with metals like zinc and magnesium, a metal salt and hydrogen gas are produced:
acid + metal → metal salt + hydrogen gas
Here, zinc reacts with hydrochloric acid and forms zinc chloride and hydrogen gas:
Zn + 2HCl → ZnCl2 + H2

subject: Science
q: A blue litmus paper will turn red when dipped into _____.
skill: Describe the properties and uses of acids
feedback: Vinegar is acidic. It contains acetic acid. When we dip blue litmus paper in any acidic solution, it turns red.

subject: Science
q: _____ is used as a preservative.
skill: Describe the properties and uses of acids
feedback: Acetic acid and citric acid are used to preserve many food items including fruit juices, syrups, jams, pickles etc.

subject: Science
q: The king of acids is _____.
skill: Describe the properties and uses of acids
feedback: Sulphuric acid is considered as the king of acids because it's the most commonly used acid. It is used in the manufacturing of many chemicals including fertilisers. It is manufactured in larger quantities than any other acid.

subject: Science
q: Which one is a strong acid?
skill: Describe the properties and uses of acids
feedback: Hydrochloric acid is an example of a strong acid. A strong acid is an acid that is completely dissociated or ionised in an aqueous solution. It has a high capacity to lose a proton, H+. Most strong acids are corrosive.

subject: Science
q: _____ is used in the manufacturing of paper.
skill: Describe the properties and uses of alkalis/bases
feedback: Sodium hydroxide is widely used in the paper and soap making industries. In the paper making industry, sodium hydroxide is used to extract cellulosic fibres from raw wood. The extracted fibres are then further processed to make the end products that are paper, wood pulp, or even furniture.

subject: Science
q: When hydrochloric acid (HCl) is neutralised by adding ammonium hydroxide (NH<sub>4</sub>OH), which salt is formed?
skill: Describe the properties and uses of alkalis/bases
feedback:
When hydrochloric acid (HCl) and ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH), which is an alkali, are mixed together, ammonium chloride salt and water are formed. This is an example of a neutralisation reaction where an acid (HCl) and an alklai (NH4OH) react to form salt (NH4Cl) and water (H2O). Its chemical reaction can be written as:
HCl + NH4OH → NH4Cl + H2O

subject: Science
q: Potassium hydroxide is used in farming to make acidic soil more alkaline so that _____.
options: soil will be saved from insects
skill: Describe the properties and uses of alkalis/bases
feedback: Potassium hydroxide (KOH) is used to reduce the acidity of soil so that plants can grow better. Plant growth and most soil processes are favoured by a soil pH range of 5.5 to 8. Acids have pH lower than 7 and alkalis have greater than 7.

subject: Science
q:
Which are the properties of alkalis?

I.   They taste bitter.
II.  They turn blue litmus paper red.
III. They are corrosive.
skill: Describe the properties and uses of alkalis/bases
feedback:
Alkalis are bitter in taste. This is because natural bitter compounds in food often contain amine groups. Amine groups are weak alkalis and bases. They come from ammonia.
Alkalis can burn skin. The colour of red litmus paper changes to blue when dipped into an alkaline solution. If you dip a blue litmus paper in an alkaline solution, the colour of the litmus paper does not change because it is already blue in colour.

subject: Science
q:
Complete the reaction.

Calcium hydroxide + Ammonium chloride → _____ + Water + _____
skill: Describe the properties and uses of alkalis/bases
feedback:
Alkalis, when heated with ammonium salt, produce ammonia gas.

We can identify ammonia gas by its strong smell. Ammonia also turns moist red litmus paper blue. Its chemical reaction can be written as:
Calcium hydroxide + Ammonium chloride → Calcium chloride + Water + Ammonia Gas
Ca(OH)2 + 2NH4Cl → CaCl2 + 2H2O + 2NH3

subject: Science
q:
Sea water is salty due to the presence of water-soluble salts such as sodium chloride (NaCl), magnesium bromide (MgBr2), etc. What other soluble salts are present in seawater?

I.   calcium sulphate (CaSO4)
II.  lead chloride (PbCl2)
III. potassium sulphate (K2 SO4)
IV. calcium carbonate (CaCO3)
skill: Describe the properties and uses of salts
feedback: Some salts are not soluble in water and they are known as insoluble salts. Seawater only contains soluble salts. Calcium sulphate (CaSO4) and potassium sulphate (K2SO4) are soluble salts, whereas lead chloride (PbCl2) and calcium carbonate (CaCO3) are insoluble salts.

subject: Science
q: Which is a salt?
skill: Describe the properties and uses of salts
feedback: KCl (potassium chloride) is a metal halite salt composed of potassium and chlorine. It is odourless and has a white or colourless crystal appearance. The solid dissolves readily in water and its solutions have a salt-like taste. However, HCl (hydrochloric acid), HNO3 (nitric acid), and H3 PO4 (phosphoric acid) are acids.

subject: Science
q: Salt is used to preserve food. The purpose of the preservation of food is to _____.
skill: Describe the properties and uses of salts
feedback: Salt is used as a preservative for food like fish and pickles. Preservation prevents them from spoiling.

subject: Science
q:
Salts are formed when

I.   acids neutralise bases.
II.  acids neutralise alkalis.
III. acids react with carbonates.
skill: Describe the properties and uses of salts
feedback: Bases that dissolve in water are known as alkalis. Acids react with bases and alkalis to form salt and water. This reaction is called neutralisation. They also form salts when they react with carbonates and bicarbonates.

subject: Science
q: If a metal hydroxide and an acid react to form potassium nitrate (KNO<sub>3</sub>), the reactants will be
skill: Describe the properties and uses of salts
feedback:
Metal hydroxide is a base and potassium nitrate is a salt. Bases react with acids and produce salts.

Potassium hydroxide (KOH) reacts with nitric acid (HNO3) to produce potassium nitrate (KNO3), which is a salt.

Its balanced chemical equation can be written as:
KOH + HNO3 → KNO3 + H2O

subject: Science
q: If bleach has a pH of 13, then it is a _____.
skill: Define pH scale and indicators, and use indicators to identify acids and alkalis
feedback: Alkalis have a pH greater than 7 and less than 14, with 8 being weakly alkaline and 14 being strongly alkaline. If bleach has a pH of 13, it is a strong alkali.

subject: Science
q: Sea water has a pH of _____.
skill: Define pH scale and indicators, and use indicators to identify acids and alkalis
feedback: The pH of seawater has remained steady for millions of years. Before the industrial era began, the average pH at the ocean surface was about 8.2 (slightly basic; 7.0 is neutral). Today it is about 8.1.

subject: Science
q: On the pH scale, a dark purple colour shows _____
skill: Define pH scale and indicators, and use indicators to identify acids and alkalis
feedback: Alkalis have a pH greater than 7 and less than 14, with 8 being weakly alkaline and 14 being extremely alkaline. Thus, dark purple represents strong alkalis as shown in the pH scale below.

subject: Science
q: On the pH scale, which of these will have a pH of 5?
skill: Define pH scale and indicators, and use indicators to identify acids and alkalis
feedback: Acids have a pH greater than 0 and less than 7, with 0 being strongly acidic and 6 being weakly acidic. Thus 5 represents weak acids.

subject: Science
q: pH is a measure of _____ ions.
skill: Define pH scale and indicators, and use indicators to identify acids and alkalis
feedback: pH is a measure of the relative amount of free hydrogen (H+) and hydroxyl ions (OH-) in water. Water that has more free hydrogen ions is acidic, whereas water that has more free hydroxyl ions is basic.

subject: Science
q: Litmus paper is used to test whether a substance is _____.
skill: Types of indicators and their colour changes - Chemistry
feedback: Litmus paper is used to indicate the acidity or alkalinity of a substance. Blue litmus paper turns red under acidic conditions, and red litmus paper turns blue under alkaline conditions.

subject: Science
q: Phenolphthalein changes colour in _____.
skill: Types of indicators and their colour changes - Chemistry
feedback: Phenolphthalein turns red or pink in alkalis, but remains colourless in acids and neutral solutions.

subject: Science
q: The colour of red cabbage remains the same in _____.
skill: Types of indicators and their colour changes - Chemistry
feedback: The purple colour of a red cabbage indicator turns red in acidic solutions and green in alkaline solutions but remains same in neutral solutions.

subject: Science
q: Plant extracts that are used to identify acids and alkalis are called _____.
skill: Types of indicators and their colour changes - Chemistry
feedback: Plant extracts which change their colour in aqueous solutions of acids or alkalis and are used to identify acids and alkalis are called natural indicators. China rose juice, red cabbage juice, turmeric, and litmus paper are commonly used natural indicators.

subject: Science
q: China rose juice turns _____ in acidic solutions.
skill: Types of indicators and their colour changes - Chemistry
feedback: China rose juice turns green in alkaline solutions and red in acidic solutions.

subject: Science
q: Turmeric juice turns _____ in an alkaline solution.
skill: Types of indicators and their colour changes - Chemistry
feedback: The yellow turmeric turns red in an alkaline solution and remains yellow in an acidic or neutral solution.

subject: Science
q: A soap solution turns red cabbage juice _____.
skill: Types of indicators and their colour changes - Chemistry
feedback: A soap solution is alkaline in nature. The purple colour of a red cabbage indicator turns red in an acidic solution and green in an alkaline solution.

subject: Science
q: A methyl orange solution turns _____ in acids.
skill: Types of indicators and their colour changes - Chemistry
feedback: Methyl orange turns red in an acidic solution and yellow in an alkaline solution.

subject: Science
q: Potassium sulphate (\[\ce{K2SO4}\]) is a salt that is produced in a reaction between _____.
skill: Define the term salts, the methods for making them, and their uses
feedback:
When potassium (\[\ce{K}\]) and sulphuric acid (\[\ce{H2SO4}\]) react with each other, they form potassium sulphate (\[\ce{K2SO4}\]) and hydrogen (\[\ce{H2}\]) gas, as shown below:

\[\ce{2K + H2SO4 -> K2SO4 + H2}\]

This is an example of a reaction between a metal and an acid that forms salt and hydrogen gas.

subject: Science
q: Sodium carbonate (\[\ce{Na2CO3}\]) is a salt that is produced in a reaction between _____.
skill: Define the term salts, the methods for making them, and their uses
feedback:
Sodium carbonate (\[\ce{Na2CO3}\]) is a salt that is produced in the reaction between sodium and carbonic acid. This is an example of a reaction between a metal and an acid that forms salt and hydrogen gas:

\[\ce{2Na + H2CO3 -> Na2CO3 + H2}\]

subject: Science
q:
Which of the following statements about salts is CORRECT?

  1. They are covalent compounds.
  2. They are made up of cations and anions.
  3. Their production requires the presence of BOTH metals and non-metals.
skill: Define the term salts, the methods for making them, and their uses
feedback: II and III only. All salts are ionic compounds that consist of metal cations and non-metal anions. For example, zinc carbonate (ZnCO3) is a salt that is made from a zinc (Zn2+) metal cation and a carbonate (CO32-) non-metal anion.

subject: Science
q: When an acid reacts with a metal carbonate, the products of the reaction are _____.
options: salt and carbon dioxide gas only
salt, carbon dioxide gas, and water
skill: Define the term salts, the methods for making them, and their uses
feedback:
The reaction between an acid and a metal carbonate leads to the formation of salt, carbon dioxide gas, and water.. For example, an acid—hydrochloric acid (HCl)—and a metal carbonate—zinc carbonate (ZnCO3)—react to form a salt—zinc chloride (ZnCl2)—carbon dioxide gas (CO2), and water (H2O):

\[\ce{2HCl + ZnCO3 -> ZnCl2 + H2O + CO2}\]

subject: Science
q: When an acid reacts with a metal oxide, the products of the reaction are _____.
skill: Define the term salts, the methods for making them, and their uses
feedback:
The reaction between an acid and a metal oxide leads to the formation of salt and water. For example, an acid—hydrochloric acid (HCl)—and a metal oxide—magnesium oxide (MgO)—react to form a salt—magnesium chloride (MgCl2), and water (H2O):

\[\ce{2HCl + MgO -> MgCl2 + H2O}\]

subject: Science
q: When an acid reacts with a metal, the products of the reaction are _____.
skill: Define the term salts, the methods for making them, and their uses
feedback:
The reaction between an acid and a metal leads to the formation of a salt and hydrogen gas. For example, an acid—hydrochloric acid (HCl)—and a metal—sodium (Na)—react to form a salt—sodium chloride (NaCl)—and hydrogen gas (H2):

\[\ce{2HCl + 2Na -> 2NaCl + H2}\]

subject: Science
q: When an acid reacts with a base, the products of the reaction are _____.
skill: Define the term salts, the methods for making them, and their uses
feedback:
The reaction between an acid and a base leads to the formation of salt and water. Such a reaction is also called neutralization reaction. For example, an acid—hydrochloric acid (HCl)—and a base—calcium hydroxide (Ca(OH)2)—react to form a salt—calcium chloride (CaCl2)—and water (H2O):

\[\ce{2HCl + Ca(OH)2 -> CaCl2 + 2H2O}\]

subject: Science
q:
Hamza's room has a window. He cannot see clearly through the window and everything outside seems blurry to him. What is TRUE about the window in his room?

I.   The glass of the window absorbs light.
II.  The glass of the window is translucent.
III. The glass of the window allows some amount of light to pass through it.
skill: Differentiate between transmission, absorption, and reflection of light - Science
feedback:
Since Hamza cannot see clearly through the glass but he can see the blurry images, the glass in his window is translucent.
A translucent material allows some of the light to pass through it and absorbs the rest of it.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following phenomena are taking place in the following image?
skill: Differentiate between transmission, absorption, and reflection of light - Science
feedback:
Transmission, absorption, and reflection are taking place in the image.
Light is transmitted through the air.
Light is absorbed by the swan and the water.
The reflection of light is taking place from the surface of water.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following statement about shadows is TRUE?
options: A shadow is formed when light is completely absorbed by an object.
A shadow is formed opposite to the source of light when light reflects from an object.
Non-luminous objects cast a shadow only when light transmits through them.
The size of the shadow depends on the distance of the object from the source of light.
skill: Differentiate between transmission, absorption, and reflection of light - Science
feedback:
Whenever light hits an opaque or translucent object, it creates a shadow.
The size of the shadow depends on the distance between the object and the source of light.
A large shadow forms if the object is near the source of light.

A shadow does not only form when light is completely absorbed or completely reflected by an object.
Most of the objects around us like walls, cars, table, and clothes, absorb some amount of light and reflect the rest. They also cast a shadow.

subject: Science
q:
We can see clearly through a clear glass. Sometimes we can also see our reflection in the same glass. Which of the following phenomena are taking place in such a condition?

I.   reflection of light
II.  transmission of light
III. absorption of light
skill: Differentiate between transmission, absorption, and reflection of light - Science
feedback:
We are able to see through a clear glass due to the transmission of light. Clear glass is a transparent object.
Sometimes we can also see our reflection in that glass. We can see this reflection because of the reflection of light.

subject: Science
q: On a sunny day, a boy wearing a black shirt feels warmer as compared to his friend who is wearing white. Why?
options: Because dark colours absorb more light than light colour.
Because light colours absorb more light than darker colours.
Because everything on the Earth absorbs equal amount of sunlight.
Because the wind energy changes into the heat when hit his clothes.
skill: Differentiate between transmission, absorption, and reflection of light - Science
feedback:
Dark colours absorb more light compared to light colours. Therefore, black absorbs more light than other colours can.
The absorbed light changes into heat, thus an object of dark colour becomes warm.

subject: Science
q:
In the given image,


the light coming out of the coloured water is different from the light coming from clear water, because
options: clear water reflects the light while coloured water transmits the light.
some portion of the light is absorbed by the coloured water.
clear water allows some of the light to pass through it.
clear water is translucent and coloured water is transparent.
skill: Differentiate between transmission, absorption, and reflection of light - Science
feedback:
In the image, the clear water is transparent while the coloured water is translucent.
Clear water allows most of the light to pass through it.
On the other hand, the coloured water allows some of the light to pass through it and absorbs rest of the light.

subject: Science
q: We are able to see ourselves in a mirror due to the
skill: Differentiate between transmission, absorption, and reflection of light - Science
feedback: We can see ourselves in a mirror because of the reflection of light. Light falls onto the shiny surface of the mirror and reflects, and we are able to see our reflection in it.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following statements about luminous objects is FALSE?
options: We get light from luminous objects.
Opaque, transparent, and translucent are types of luminous objects.
All stars in the galaxy are luminous.
Light comes out from luminous objects.
skill: Differentiate between transmission, absorption, and reflection of light - Science
feedback:
Opaque, transparent, and translucent objects are types of non-luminous objects.
The objects that give out light are called luminous objects.
All stars, including the sun, are also luminous.

subject: Science
q: The mirror you see in your bathroom is classified as a _____ mirror.
skill: Describe the characteristics of images formed by plane mirrors with examples - Science
feedback: The mirrors used in bathrooms show an image of the same size as the object in front of them. Forming an image of the same size and shape is a characteristic of a plane mirror. Therefore, a bathroom mirror is a plane mirror.

subject: Science
q:
Which of the following figures represents the image of the pencil formed by a plane mirror?

I.
II.
III.
skill: Describe the characteristics of images formed by plane mirrors with examples - Science
feedback: The image formed by a plane mirror is a duplicate of an object placed in front of it. It is of the same size and shape and is always virtual and erect.

subject: Science
q: The figure shows image formation by a plane mirror. If the size of the flame is 2 cm, then what is the size of the flame in the image?
options: half the size of the flame
double the size of the flame
equal to the size of the flame
three times the size of the flame
skill: Describe the characteristics of images formed by plane mirrors with examples - Science
feedback: The image formed by a plane mirror is a duplicate of the object placed in front of it. Therefore, the size of the flame in the image has the same size as the actual flame. It will be equal to 2 cm.

subject: Science
q: A man stands one metre away from a plane mirror. How far away does the man seem to be from his image?
skill: Describe the characteristics of images formed by plane mirrors with examples - Science
feedback:
The image of a man standing one metre away from a plane mirror will appear to be two metres away from him. This is because the distance between the image and the mirror appears to be the same as the distance between the object and the mirror.
>

subject: Science
q: The height of an image appearing in a plane mirror is _____ the height of the actual object.
skill: Describe the characteristics of images formed by plane mirrors with examples - Science
feedback: The image formed by a plane mirror is a duplicate of an object placed in front of it. Therefore, it has the same size as the actual object.

subject: Science
q: A plane mirror produces an image which is _____.
skill: Describe the characteristics of images formed by plane mirrors with examples - Science
feedback: A plane mirror produces an image which is reversed from left to right, which means it is laterally inverted. Laterally inverted means that the left side of the object appears to be the right of its image and the right side of the object appears to be the left of its image.

subject: Science
q: What is true about the image formed by a plane mirror?
options: The distance of the image from the mirror appears to be the same as the distance of the object from the mirror.
It appears smaller than the object placed in front of the mirror.
It is virtual and larger than the object itself.
skill: Describe the characteristics of images formed by plane mirrors with examples - Science
feedback: The virtual image formed by the plane mirror appears to be behind the mirror. The distance between this image and the mirror appears to be the same as the distance between the mirror and the object itself.

subject: Science
q: The light waves are usually represented by
skill: Demonstrate the law of reflection
feedback: The light waves are represented by a straight arrow.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following statements about the reflection of light is TRUE?
options: The reflection of light takes place through transparent objects.
The law of reflection can be observed when light falls onto a mirror.
The reflection of light takes place when light falls onto luminous objects.
We can see our reflection on a wall.
skill: Demonstrate the law of reflection
feedback:
The law of reflection can be observed by looking at a mirror.
For example, while looking in the mirror, if we move from left to right, we are able to see things on the opposite side.

The transmission of light takes place through transparent objects. While in the reflection of light and absorption of light take place through opaque and translucent objects.

subject: Science
q: An incident ray falls on a surface along the normal, i.e. making an angle of 90\[^{\circ}\] to the surface. Which of the following will be the reflected ray in such a case?
skill: Demonstrate the law of reflection
feedback:
The red ray will be the reflected ray.
The incident ray is along the normal line, that is, the angle of incidence is 0\[^{\circ}\].
According to the law of reflection of light, the angle of reflection is equal to the angle of incidence.
Thus, the angle of reflection will be 0\[^{\circ}\] and the reflected ray will be along the normal.

subject: Science
q:
If the incident ray makes an angle of 30\[^{\circ}\] with the surface on which the incident ray falls,


then the angle of reflection is
skill: Demonstrate the law of reflection
feedback:
The angle between the incident ray and the plane is 30\[^{\circ}\].
So, the angle of incidence = 90\[^{\circ}\]-30\[^{\circ}\]=60\[^{\circ}\].
According to the law of reflection,
angle of reflection = angle of incidence.

So, angle of reflection = 60\[^{\circ}\].

subject: Science
q: When the light falls onto the surface of a mirror,
options: it is absorbed into the mirror.
skill: Demonstrate the law of reflection
feedback:
Rays of light are reflected when they fall onto the surface of a mirror.
The reflection does not make the mirror luminous.

Transparent objects allow light to pass through. The mirror is not a transparent object.

subject: Science
q:
Which of the following are examples of the law of reflection of light?

I.   seeing the cars behind through the rear-view mirrors of a car
II.  looking at yourself in a mirror
III. seeing an image of yourself in a spoon
skill: Demonstrate the law of reflection
feedback: Seeing the cars behind through the rear-view mirror of a car, looking at yourself in a mirror, and seeing an image of yourself in a spoon are all examples of the reflection of light.

subject: Science
q: An incident ray makes an angle of 35\[^{\circ}\] with the surface on which the light falls. The angle of incidence is _____.
skill: Demonstrate the law of reflection
feedback:
The angle that the incident ray makes with the surface is 35\[^{\circ}\]. The angle between the surface and the normal line is 90\[^{\circ}\]. So the angle of incidence, that is the angle between the incident ray and the normal ray, is calculated by
90\[^{\circ}\] - 35\[^{\circ}\] = 55\[^{\circ}\].

subject: Science
q:
In the image,


if the torch is moved towards the normal line, then the angle of reflection
skill: Demonstrate the law of reflection
feedback:
As the torch is moved towards the normal, the angle of incidence decreases.
As the angle of incidence decreases, the angle of reflection also decreases.

subject: Science
q: How does a solar cooker cook food?
options: By converging all the light rays at the focal point.
By using concave mirrors in combination with plane mirrors.
By diverging all the light rays away from the focal point.
skill: Describe the characteristics of images formed by concave mirrors with examples - Science
feedback: A solar cooker uses a concave mirror. We already know that parallel rays of light converge at focal point after reflection. When sun rays strike concave mirrors, they converge at the focal point. This makes the focal point very hot point. If we put a paper onto this point, it would burn. We can use this focal point for cooking or heating up food.

subject: Science
q: The headlights of automobiles and flashlights use _____ mirrors.
skill: Describe the characteristics of images formed by concave mirrors with examples - Science
feedback: Light travels in straight lines. If we place a light bulb at the focal point of a concave mirror, then almost all the rays from the bulb will travel in one direction after reflection. This is something which we use in the headlights of an automobile.

subject: Science
q: What is NOT true about concave mirrors?
options: They are used in the headlights of automobiles.
They are used by the dentists to examine the tooth of a patient.
Their image depends on the position of an objects in front of the mirror.
skill: Describe the characteristics of images formed by concave mirrors with examples - Science
feedback:
Concave mirrors can form both upright and inverted images. This is why their image is not always upright.

Whether an image is virtual or real, and upright or inverted depends on the position of the object in front of the mirror.

subject: Science
q: A light ray strikes the surface of a concave mirror. Which of the following represents the reflected ray?
skill: Describe the characteristics of images formed by concave mirrors with examples - Science
feedback:
When a light ray strikes the surface of a concave mirror, it gets deflected at a certain angle.

In this figure, the blue ray strikes the mirror and is deflected to a point beyond 'f'.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following images can be viewed on a screen?
skill: Describe the characteristics of images formed by concave mirrors with examples - Science
feedback: A real image can be viewed on a screen. This is because the light rays that strike the surface of a concave mirror are reflected in such a way that they converge at one point, the focal point.

subject: Science
q: Dentists use a _____ to focus light onto the tooth of a patient.
skill: Describe the characteristics of images formed by concave mirrors with examples - Science
feedback: Dentists use a concave mirror to focus light onto the tooth of a patient. This is because a concave mirror shows a magnified image of the tooth.

subject: Science
q: If the image formed by a concave mirror is virtual, upright, and magnified, then an object is placed ______
skill: Describe the characteristics of images formed by concave mirrors with examples - Science
feedback:
When an object is placed closer to the mirror, the image is magnified, virtual, and upright. This is because the light rays diverge after reflection.
So, a virtual image is formed behind the mirror.

subject: Science
q: A mirror that shows smaller images of large objects is called a _____.
skill: Describe the characteristics of images formed by convex mirrors with examples - Science
feedback: Convex mirrors form small images. Therefore, a wider view of the area can be seen with the help of a convex mirror. Convex mirrors are used as rear-view mirrors in cars and in blind-corner mirrors on roads.

subject: Science
q: What type of mirror is used in supermarkets for security purposes?
skill: Describe the characteristics of images formed by convex mirrors with examples - Science
feedback: Convex mirrors form small images. Therefore, a wider view of the area can be seen with the help of a convex mirror. This allows supermarket employees to keep an eye on a large area of the market.

subject: Science
q: The focal point lies behind the mirror in _____ mirrors.
skill: Describe the characteristics of images formed by convex mirrors with examples - Science
feedback: Light rays that fall on a convex mirror diverge after reflection. The incident light rays that originate from one point reflect of the surface of the mirror and diverge. The diverged light rays meet at a common point behind the mirror, called a focal point.

subject: Science
q: A spherical mirror with its reflecting surface curved outwards is called a _____ mirror.
skill: Describe the characteristics of images formed by convex mirrors with examples - Science
feedback: A mirror with a reflecting surface that bulges outwards is a convex mirror.

subject: Science
q: A convex mirror produces a virtual image when an object is _____
options: anywhere in front of the mirror.
skill: Describe the characteristics of images formed by convex mirrors with examples - Science
feedback: A convex mirror always forms a virtual image of the object placed anywhere in front of it. When light rays parallel to the principal axis strike a convex mirror, they reflect and spread in such a way that they appear to come from a point behind the mirror.

subject: Science
q: What is NOT correct about a convex mirror?
options: It always gives an upright image.
It always gives a real image.
It always gives a virtual image.
It always gives a small image.
skill: Describe the characteristics of images formed by convex mirrors with examples - Science
feedback: The image formed by a convex mirror is always upright and virtual. When light rays parallel to principal axis strike a convex mirror, they reflect and spread in such a way that they appear to come from a point behind the mirror. Such images are virtual. When we call an image virtual, we mean an image that cannot be captured on photographic paper or film. However, a real image seems to be in front of the mirror, and can be captured on a photographic paper or film.

subject: Science
q:
Convex mirrors are used as blind-corner mirrors because
I. Their image is always upright.
II. Their image is always large.
III. They have a wide field of view.
skill: Describe the characteristics of images formed by convex mirrors with examples - Science
feedback: A convex mirror gives a wider view of a road. Also, the image formed by a convex mirror is always upright. However, the objects in the mirror look smaller than they really are. They're placed at blind corners of roads, so that drivers can see vehicles and other objects around the corner.

subject: Science
q: A light ray strikes the surface of a convex mirror as shown in the figure. Which of the following shows the reflected ray?
skill: Describe the characteristics of images formed by convex mirrors with examples - Science
feedback: When a light ray parallel to the principal axis strikes a convex mirror, it reflects and spreads in such a way that it appears to come from a point behind the mirror. The light ray is converged towards the focal point behind the mirror and is diverged away from the reflecting surface of the mirror.

subject: Science
q: Identify the colour X in the rainbow:
skill: Describe the dispersion and spectrum of light through a prism - Science
feedback: Red is the outermost colour of the rainbow, and labelled as X in the diagram. The colours of the rainbow will always be in the same order and can be remembered with the mnemonic VIBGYOR from bottom up. The 'R' in the VIBGYOR stands for red.

subject: Science
q: _____ is/are a tiny part of the _____.
skill: Describe the dispersion and spectrum of light through a prism - Science
feedback:
The light spectrum, also called the visible spectrum, is a tiny part of the electromagnetic spectrum.
UV radiation is a part of the electromagnetic (EM) spectrum, while sound waves are longitudinal waves and therefore not a part of the EM spectrum.

subject: Science
q: Light is a form of
skill: Describe the dispersion and spectrum of light through a prism - Science
feedback: Light is a form of energy that travels in waves.

subject: Science
q:
Which of the following waves are invisible to the naked eye?
I. infrared waves
II. red light waves
III. ultraviolet waves
IV. violet light waves
skill: Describe the dispersion and spectrum of light through a prism - Science
feedback:
Infrared and ultraviolet waves are the invisible part of the electromagnetic spectrum. This means that humans cannot see them with their naked eyes.

However, red and violet waves are part of the spectrum of light, which is the only part of the electromagnetic spectrum that is visible to the naked eye.

subject: Science
q: White light is a mixture of _____ colours.
skill: Describe the dispersion and spectrum of light through a prism - Science
feedback: Light or visible light is a combination of seven colours: red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo and violet. When visible light passes through a prism, it splits into different colours.

subject: Science
q: What is TRUE about the spectrum of light?
options: It is the splitting of white light into different colours.
It is the band of colours obtained from the dispersion of light.
It is a range of different wavelengths of electromagnetic radiations.
It is a band of primary colours only.
skill: Describe the dispersion and spectrum of light through a prism - Science
feedback:
The spectrum of light is a band of seven colours resulting from the dispersion of light.
The splitting of white light into different colours is called dispersion of light.
The range of different electromagnetic radiations is called an electromagnetic spectrum.
The colours of the spectrum of light comprise more than just the primary colours.

subject: Science
q: The splitting of _____ light into its component colours is called ______.
skill: Describe the dispersion and spectrum of light through a prism - Science
feedback: When white light passes through a prism, it bends and splits into different colours. This splitting of this white light into its component colours is called dispersion of light.

subject: Science
q: On what basis do we see the colour of an object?
options: The colour of the light shone on it.
The colour of the light that is absorbed.
The colour depends on the object entirely.
The colour of the light it reflects.
skill: Explain why an opaque object appears to be of a certain colour
feedback: The colour of an object is decided by the colour of the light it reflects.

subject: Science
q: If you were building a house in a warm place, what colour would you paint it to keep the house cool?
options: Paint it black, so light and heat are reflected.
Paint it white, so light and heat are reflected
Paint it black, so light and heat are absorbed.
Paint it white, so light and heat are absorbed.
skill: Explain why an opaque object appears to be of a certain colour
feedback: Heat energy travels with light energy. Therefore, in a hot place, most houses are painted in light colours so that light is reflected. This way, less heat is absorbed and the houses remain cool.

subject: Science
q: What is true about the colours of Pakistan's flag?
options: All the colours are reflected for the white part, whereas the green part only reflects the colour green.
The colour white is reflected for the white part, and the colour green for the green part.
The colour white is absorbed for the white part, and the colour green for the green part.
All the colours are absorbed for the white part, and only the colour green is absorbed for the green part.
skill: Explain why an opaque object appears to be of a certain colour
feedback: Pakistan's flag is green and white. In order to see the colour white, all colours of the spectrum are reflected. Whereas to see the colour green, all the colours are absorbed except for green. Green is reflected.

subject: Science
q: What colours are being absorbed in the given image?
skill: Explain why an opaque object appears to be of a certain colour
feedback: The image is of a carrot. Since the carrot is orange and green, this means all colours except orange and green are being absorbed. The colour of an object is shown by the colour or colours it reflects. A carrot reflects orange and green.

subject: Science
q: Amna, Sana, and Aisha shine their torches on one spot and see that white light forms there. What colour are their torches' lights?
skill: Explain why an opaque object appears to be of a certain colour
feedback: Red, blue, and green are primary colours. All other colours are produced from the combination of these colours.  Therefore, when these colours combine they make white.

subject: Science
q: What happens to light when it hits an opaque object?
skill: Explain why an opaque object appears to be of a certain colour
feedback: An opaque object does not allow light to pass through it. When light strikes an opaque object, it is either absorbed or reflected.

subject: Science
q: Why can't you see inside an orange?
skill: Explain why an opaque object appears to be of a certain colour
feedback: Opacity hides what is within an object because light reflects off of the object. The reflecting property gives the object its colour. Therefore, we cannot see through an opaque object or inside it.

subject: Science
q: Why do we wear white or light-coloured clothes in summer?
options: Because they reflect light and heat.
skill: Explain why an opaque object appears to be of a certain colour
feedback: A white object appears white because it reflects all the light. Light and heat energy travel together, which is why when light is reflected, less heat energy is absorbed.

subject: Science
q: Which is the CORRECT formula of refractive index?
options: \[\text{Refractive index} = {\text{speed of light in a vacuum}} \times {\text{speed of light in the medium}}\]
\[\begin{align}\text {Refractive index}  &= \frac{\text{speed of light in a vacuum}}{\text{speed of light in the medium}} \\\\\end{align}\]
\[\begin{align}\text {Refractive index}  &= \frac{\text{speed of light in the medium}}{\text{speed of light in a vacuum}} \\\\\end{align}\]
\[\text{Speed of light in a vacuum} = {\text{refractive index}} \times {\text{speed of light in the medium}}\]
skill: Explain the refraction of light and its causes
feedback:
A refractive index is the ratio of the speed of light in a vacuum to its speed in the medium.

\[\begin{align}\text {Refractive index}  &= \frac{\text{speed of light in a vacuum}}{\text{speed of light in the medium}} \\\\\end{align}\].

It is represented by the symbol \[\eta\]

subject: Science
q: If a light ray falls perpendicular to the surface of a dense medium,
options: it changes its direction but not speed.
it changes speed as well as direction.
it changes its speed, but not the direction.
it does not change direction or speed.
skill: Explain the refraction of light and its causes
feedback: If the incident ray falls normally to the surface of a dense medium like glass, then there is no bending of the ray of light and it goes straight as shown below.

subject: Science
q: When a ray of light enters a dense medium from a rare medium, it bends towards the
skill: Explain the refraction of light and its causes
feedback: According to the law of refraction, when a ray of light enters a dense medium from a rare medium, it bends towards the normal. Notice the bending of the refracted ray shown through CD.

subject: Science
q: When a ray of light enters a rare medium from a dense medium, it bends away from the
skill: Explain the refraction of light and its causes
feedback: According to the law of refraction, when a ray of light enters a rare medium from a dense medium, it bends away from the normal, shown by AB in the figure below.

subject: Science
q: When light passes from one transparent medium to another, it changes its _____ and _____.
skill: Explain the refraction of light and its causes
feedback: When light passes from one transparent medium to another, it changes its direction and speed. These changes take place because of the different densities of the two media.

subject: Science
q: Light travels in a/an _____ line.
skill: Explain the refraction of light and its causes
feedback: Light travels in a straight line. If an object is placed in front of a light source, the light is blocked as it cannot curl or zig zag around the object.

subject: Science
q: A stick immersed in water looks bent due to the _____ of light.
skill: Explain the refraction of light and its causes
feedback:
When a stick is dipped partly in water, it appears to be broken at the intersection of water and air. This is due to the property of refraction of light.

The speed of light is different in different mediums. There are rarer and denser mediums. A rarer medium is lighter, such as air. Water and glass are examples of dense mediums. The speed of light is slow in denser and fast in rarer mediums. Therefore, when light passes from a dense into a rare medium, it bends due to the difference in speed.

subject: Science
q: Which scenario is shown in the figure?
skill: Explain the refraction of light and its causes
feedback: Glass is a dense medium and air is a rare medium. According to the law of refraction, when a ray of light enters a rare medium from a dense medium, it bends away from the normal. Instead of going along the dotted line the ray of light moves away from the normal. This results in a greater angle of refraction (r) than the angle of incidence (i).

subject: Science
q:
Which of the following statements are TRUE regarding the law of refraction?

I.    The incident ray, the refracted ray, and the normal at the point of incidence all lie in the same plane.
II.   The angle of incidence and the angle of refraction are always equal.
III. The ratio of the speed of light in a vacuum to its speed in another medium is always constant.
skill: Explain the refraction of light and its causes
feedback:
There are two laws of refraction.

1. The incident ray, the refracted ray, and the normal at the point of incidence, all lie in the same plane.
2. The ratio of the speed of light in vacuum to its speed in another medium is always constant.

When a ray of light passes from a rare medium to a dense medium, \[\angle r< \angle i\]  and vice versa. So, statement II is not correct.

Hence, statements I and III are correct.

subject: Science
q: Which figure shows the refraction of light when a light ray enters a dense medium from a rare medium?
skill: Explain the refraction of light and its causes
feedback:
Observe that glass is a dense medium, and air is a rare medium. According to the law of refraction, when a ray of light enters a dense medium from a rare medium, it bends towards the normal. Instead of going along the dotted line, the ray of light moves closer to the normal. In other words, the angle of refraction (r) is greater than the angle of incidence (i). This refraction is shown visually as:



This process reverses when a ray of light travels from a dense medium (glass) to a rare medium (air).

subject: Science
q: The angle between a normal and a refracted ray is called the
skill: Explain the refraction of light and its causes
feedback: The angle of refraction is the angle between a normal and a refracted ray. In the figure below, angle r is the angle of refraction.

subject: Science
q: Calculate the speed of light in a diamond if the refractive index is \[2.4\].
skill: Explain the refraction of light and its causes
feedback:
The refractive index is given by the formula:

\[\text {Refractive index} = \frac{\text{speed of light in a vacuum}}{\text{speed of light in a diamond}}\].

Since the speed of light in air is almost equal to the speed of light in the vacuum, we replace the values of the speed of light in a vacuum with the speed of light in air.

By putting values into the formula, we get:

\[\eta = \frac{\text{speed of light in the air}}{\text{speed of light in the diamond}}\]

\[2.4 = \frac{3\times 10^{8} \text{ms}^{-1}}{\text{speed of light in the diamond }}\]


\[2.4 \times {\text{speed of light in a diamond }} = {3\times 10^{8} \text{ms}^{-1}}\]


\[{\text{speed of light in a diamond }} = \frac{3\times 10^{8} \text{ms}^{-1}}{\text{2.4 }}\]


\[{\text{speed of light in a diamond }} = 1.25 \times 10^{8}\text{ms}^{-1} \]

subject: Science
q:
Find the refractive index of the glass if the speed of light through it is \[2 \times 10^{8} \text{ms}^{-1}\].
(Note that the speed of light in air is \[3 \times 10^{8} \text{ms}^{-1}\].)
skill: Explain the refraction of light and its causes
feedback:
A refractive index is found by the formula:

\[\text {Refractive index} = \frac{\text{speed of light in a vacuum}}{\text{speed of light in the medium}}\].

Since the speed of light in air is almost equal to the speed of light in the vacuum, we replace the values of the speed of light in a vacuum with the speed of light in air.

By putting values into this formula, we get:

\[\eta = \frac{\text{speed of light in a vacuum}}{\text{speed of light in the medium}}\].

\[\eta = \frac{3\times 10^{8} \text{ms}^{-1}}{2\times 10^{8} \text{ms}^{-1}}\].


\[\eta = \frac{3}{2}\]

\[\eta = 1.5\]

skill: Explain the refraction of light and its causes

When a stick is dipped partly in water, it appears to be broken at the intersection of water and air. This is due to the property of refraction of light.
