subject: Science
q: The ratio of the speed of light in a vacuum to the speed of light in a material is called
skill: Explain the refraction of light and its causes
feedback:
The ratio of the speed of light in a vacuum to the speed of light in a material is called the refractive index of the material.
When light enters from one medium to another, it bends slightly from its original path. This bending of light is called refraction.
Reflection is the bouncing back of light when it hits an object. The splitting of a ray of light into seven colours after passing through a prism is called the dispersion of light.

subject: Science
q: When light enters from one medium to another, it bends slightly from its original path. This bending of light is called
skill: Explain the refraction of light and its causes
feedback:
When light enters from one medium to another, it bends slightly from its original path. This bending of light is called refraction.
Reflection is light bouncing back when it hits an object. The splitting of a ray of light into seven colours after passing through a prism is called the dispersion of light. Rotation refers to the rotation of an object about an axis or centre.

subject: Science
q: The severity of a shock depends on
options: the path of a current passing through the body.
the amount of a current passing through the body.
the duration of an electric shock.
the path, amount, and duration of a current passing through the body.
skill: Describe the dangers of electricity, and safety measures that can be taken
feedback:
The severity of an electric shock depends on all three factors:
The path of a current passing through the body: the smaller the path the more severe the shock.
The amount of a current: the larger the current, the more severe the shock.
The duration of an electric shock: the longer the duration, the more severe the shock.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following is NOT a safety measure in the use of electricity?
options: Do not change a light bulb while it is switched off.
Do not touch a plug or switch with wet hands.
Do not try to insert any metal object into electrical sockets.
Do not use wires that are damaged or exposed.
skill: Describe the dangers of electricity, and safety measures that can be taken
feedback: It is a safety measure to turn off the switch of the light bulb before changing it. It is dangerous to change a light bulb while its switch is still on.

subject: Science
q: Identify a danger of electricity with reference to the image below:
options: Flying a kite in an open field can cause a short circuit in the electricity line.
The house is located dangerously close to the transformer.
Flying a kite in an open field can cause overheating of the power lines.
Flying a kite too close to the power lines can result in an electric shock.
skill: Describe the dangers of electricity, and safety measures that can be taken
feedback:
Flying a kite too close to the power lines may cause an electric shock.
The metallic wire of the kite is a conductor. If the kite touches the power line, high current may flow down the wire through the person and into the ground, thus completing a circuit and causing an electric shock.

subject: Science
q: Ali was trying to plug a faulty wire into the socket barefoot, and he received an electric shock. He then put on rubber shoes and tried to put in the faulty wire again. This time, he did not receive a shock. Why?
options: The rubber shoes prevented the leakage of the current from the faulty wires.
The path of the flow of a current is complete after putting on rubber shoes.
The human body is a good conductor of electricity, and rubber is an insulator of electricity.
An electric shock can only be received when a person is barefoot.
skill: Describe the dangers of electricity, and safety measures that can be taken
feedback:
Handling faulty wires can cause a leakage of a large current through the body and result in an electric shock.
When he was barefoot, Ali's body acted as a conductor to the excess current, and he received an electric shock.
However, when he wore rubber shoes, the rubber acted as an insulator to the excess current, and Ali did not receive any shock.

subject: Science
q: What electrical hazard is shown below?
options: a damaged insulation that can cause an electric shock
an electricity leakage caused due to water
an increase in the brightness of both bulbs
overloading as too many appliances are plugged into one switch
skill: Describe the dangers of electricity, and safety measures that can be taken
feedback: Plugging too many appliances into a single socket is called overloading. Overloading causes a large current to pass through the wires, thus causing overheating.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following statements is FALSE about safety devices?
options: MCBs work like fuses but they do not blow out.
A three-pin plug can only be inserted in a three-pin socket.
Fuses are made up of conducting wires with a low melting point.
An earth wire connects an appliance directly to the main switch.
skill: Describe the dangers of electricity, and safety measures that can be taken
feedback:
An earth wire does not connect an appliance to the main switch. It connects the metal casing of an appliance to the ground or earth. Hence, the name earth wire.
The wire that connects an appliance directly to the main switch is called a live wire.

subject: Science
q: ELCB stands for _____, and it is a/an _____.
options: Electrical Leakage Circuit Breaker; electrical appliance
Earth Leakage Circuit Breaker; safety device
Electrical Leakage Circuit Breaker; safety device
Earth Leakage Circuit Breaker; electrical insulator
skill: Describe the dangers of electricity, and safety measures that can be taken
feedback: ELCB stands for Earth Leakage Circuit Breaker, and it is a safety device that breaks the circuit when a current exceeds the safe limit.

subject: Science
q: How does an earth wire work?
options: Earth wires send an excess amount of current into the ground.
Earth wires melt and burn out when a current exceeds the safe limit.
Earth wires trip and break the circuit when a current exceeds the safe limit.
Earth wires store an excess amount of current flowing through the appliances.
skill: Describe the dangers of electricity, and safety measures that can be taken
feedback:
Earth wires protect us from electric shocks by sending an excess amount of current into the ground. If short circuiting occurs in a device, the current will flow directly into the earth through the earth wire.
Fuses melt and burn out when a current exceeds the safe limit.
Circuit breakers trip and break the circuit when a current exceeds the safe limit.

subject: Science
q: MCBs are better than fuses because _____
options: they use earth wires to connect appliances to the earth.
they do not break the circuit when the current is larger than normal.
they do not need to be replaced again and again as they don't burn out.
they do not stop the flow of a current.
skill: Describe the dangers of electricity, and safety measures that can be taken
feedback:
The disadvantage of a fuse is that it needs to be replaced manually. To overcome this problem, automatic fuses called miniature circuit breakers or MCBs are used.
Whenever there is an excess amount of current flow in the circuit, it automatically switches off, breaking the circuit. It does not burn out and can simply be turned back on.

subject: Science
q: A short circuit can result in _____.
skill: Describe the dangers of electricity, and safety measures that can be taken
feedback: Short circuits occur when <u>wires form a path of least resistance</u>. The circuit path for the flow of a current becomes smaller, thus <u>overheating the wires</u>. This overheating of the conducting wires can also result in a fire.

subject: Science
q: What is true about a short circuit?
options: It is a shock received by a person when a light bulb is turned on.
It is the overheating of wires when a large current passes through the circuit.
It is the flow of a current through a person's body.
It is a circuit consisting of only one bulb.
skill: Describe the dangers of electricity, and safety measures that can be taken
feedback:
A short circuit occurs when wires accidentally touch, forming a closed loop of very low resistance. This draws a large current from the source, overheating the wires.
The flow of a current through a person's body is called an electric shock.

subject: Science
q: The image below shows the wiring of a three-pin plug. What is correct about wires A, B and C?
options: A = live wire; B = neutral wire; C = earth wire
A = earth wire; B = neutral wire; C = live wire
A = neutral wire; B = live wire; C = earth wire
A = live wire; B = earth wire; C = neutral wire
skill: Describe the dangers of electricity, and safety measures that can be taken
feedback:
The three-pin plug has three wires fit into it.
The blue wire is the neutral wire; the green wire is the earth wire, and the brown wire is the live wire. The live and the neutral wires are connected to the main switch, whereas the earth wire is connected to the ground.

subject: Science
q: What is true about an electric shock?
options: It is a shock received by a person when they switch on the lights.
It is the overheating of wires when a large current passes through the circuit.
It is the flow of a current through a person's body.
It is the sparks released from a faulty switch.
skill: Describe the dangers of electricity, and safety measures that can be taken
feedback:
An electric shock occurs when a person touches live or damaged wires, causing excess current to flow through his body.
The overheating of wires and the sparks from a switch are signs of a short circuit.

subject: Science
q: An earth wire is used _____.
options: to prevent the flow of currents
to protect appliances from fluctuating voltages
in electrical wiring of the floors
skill: Describe the dangers of electricity, and safety measures that can be taken
feedback: The metal body of an electrical appliance is earthed to avoid the risk of electric shocks. The excess current flows down into the earth.

subject: Science
q: A fuse is used to _____.
options: prevent the flow of excessive current
skill: Describe the dangers of electricity, and safety measures that can be taken
feedback: The function of a fuse is to prevent the flow of excessive current in a circuit. Fuses have a low melting point. In case of an excessive current, the wire in the fuse melts, thus breaking the circuit.

subject: Science
q: A hammer is exerting the pressure of 30 Pa on a nail with a surface area of 3 m<sup>2</sup>. What would be the force?
skill: Define pressure and its units
feedback:
Recall that pressure is force divided by area.

Pressure = Force/Area

So, force can be calculated by finding the product of pressure and area.

Force = Pressure x Area

= 30 Pa x 3 m2
or
= 30 N/m2 x 3 m-2
F = 90 N

subject: Science
q: Calculate the pressure produced by a force of 800 N acting on an area of 2.0 m<sup>2</sup> .
skill: Define pressure and its units
feedback:
Pressure is defined as force per unit area or P = F/A

P = (800 N)/(2m2)
P = 400 N/m2
or
F = 400 Pa

subject: Science
q: Which action requires less force?
options: cutting fruits with a rounded edge knife
skill: Define pressure and its units
feedback: A lightweight ball has a smaller surface area and weighs less as compared to a heavy, larger ball, so kicking it requires less force.

subject: Science
q: An elephant weighing 20,000 N stands on an area of 1000 cm<sup>2</sup>. How much pressure would it exert on the ground?
skill: Define pressure and its units
feedback:
Force applied by the elephant= F = 20,000 N
Area = A = 1000 cm2

Note that the area is in centimeters, we need to convert it into meters.
1 m = 100 cm, so
= 1000/100 x 100m2 = 0.1 m2

The pressure is given by

Pressure = P = F/A

= 20,000 N/0.1m2
P = 2,00,000 N/m2

subject: Science
q: A sharp knife can cut through an object easily because _____.
options: a small force exerts a large pressure on its narrow edge
no pressure is exerted on the edge
skill: Define pressure and its units
feedback: A sharp knife has a thin edge so even if we apply force over a small area of the knife, it will still create pressure and cut the object easily.

subject: Science
q: A force of 30 N acted over an area of 3 m<sup>2</sup>. What is the pressure?
skill: Define pressure and its units
feedback:
Pressure is defined as force per unit area or P = F/A

P = (30 N)/(3m2)

P = 10 N/m2
or
P = 10 Pa

subject: Science
q: The pressure on the surface of an object increases by _____.
skill: Define pressure and its units
feedback: Pressure is inversely proportional to the area of a surface. If we decrease the surface area of an object by keeping the force constant, the pressure will increase and vice versa.

subject: Science
q: Pressure is equal to _____.
skill: Define pressure and its units
feedback:
Pressure is equal to force divided by area.

Pressure = Force/Area
P = F/A

subject: Science
q: Pressure in liquids acts in _____.
skill: Explain water pressure
feedback: Pressure in liquids acts equally in all directions.

subject: Science
q: If we make holes at the base of the water container, from which hole/holes will water flow be the greatest?
skill: Explain water pressure
feedback: Pressure in liquids increases with an increase in depth. At the bottom of the water container, pressure is highest so water flow from the fourth and fifth hole will be greatest.

subject: Science
q: From which hole will water flow out of at the widest angle?
skill: Explain water pressure
feedback: Pressure in liquids increases with an increase in depth. So, from the third hole, water will flow at the widest angle.

subject: Science
q: Which layer has the least pressure?
skill: Explain water pressure
feedback: Notice that the second layer of water is bearing the weight of the first layer. Similarly, the third layer is bearing the weight of the first as well as the second layer. The fourth layer is bearing the weight of the top three layers and so on. So, clearly the top layer has the least pressure.

subject: Science
q: In the water container below, which layer is bearing the pressure of all other layers?
skill: Explain water pressure
feedback: Observe that the second layer of water is bearing the weight of the first layer. Similarly, the third layer is bearing the weight of the first as well as the second layer. The fourth layer is bearing the weight of the top three layers. The fifth layer is bearing the weight of all the layers.

subject: Science
q: Water pressure on the base of the container will be _____.
skill: Explain water pressure
feedback: Water pressure flows from an upward to a downward direction and it increases with an increase in depth. On the base of the container, water pressure will be downwards and higher.

subject: Science
q: As you go deeper under the sea, the pressure of water pushing down on you is _____.
skill: Explain water pressure
feedback: This is due to an increase in hydrostatic pressure, the force per unit area exerted by a liquid on an object. The deeper you go under the sea, the greater the pressure of the water pushing down on you.

subject: Science
q: What happens when the plunger of a syringe is pulled back?
skill: Explain behaviour and applications of gases under pressure
feedback: When the plunger of the syringe is pulled back, you are increasing the volume in the container and reducing the pressure. They are inversely proportional, and one decreases when the other increases. The liquid draws up into the syringe to balance the pressure, making it equal to the pressure outside of the syringe.

subject: Science
q:
Which are applications of gases under pressure?

I.   a vehicle lifter
II.  air brakes
III. a vacuum pump
skill: Explain behaviour and applications of gases under pressure
feedback: Pneumatics is a part of technology that deals with the study and application of pressurised gas to produce mechanical motion. Applications of pneumatics include air brakes and a vacuum pump.

subject: Science
q: If pressure is decreased, gases are free to move about in a _____.
skill: Explain behaviour and applications of gases under pressure
feedback: Gas molecules keep moving and remain a certain distance apart from one another. An increase in pressure pushes the molecules closer together, reducing the volume. If the pressure is decreased, the gases are free to move about in a larger volume.

subject: Science
q: A sample of hydrogen gas has a volume of 10 L and a pressure of 2 atm. What is the new pressure if the volume is decreased to 5 L at a constant temperature?
skill: Explain behaviour and applications of gases under pressure
feedback: This is a numerical problem of Boyle's law. It can be seen from the question that the volume is 10 L at a pressure of 2 atm. The volume is then decreased to 5 L, which means half of 10 L. So, its pressure will be doubled, that is, 4 atm.

subject: Science
q: Decreasing the volume of a closed container of gas causes the pressure to _____.
skill: Explain behaviour and applications of gases under pressure
feedback: According to Boyle's law, if the volume of gas decreases at a constant temperature then the pressure increases because they have an inversely proportional relationship.

subject: Science
q: Popping a balloon uses which law?
skill: Explain behaviour and applications of gases under pressure
feedback: When popping a balloon, you are attempting to reduce the amount of air trapped inside the container. Thus, you increase the pressure on the system. You squeeze the balloon, increasing the pressure, which decreases the volume. The balance of the amount of air or gas and the filling capacity of the balloon is disturbed. In order to restore balance, the balloon must burst. This is an example of Boyle's law.

subject: Science
q: At a constant temperature, what happens to pressure when the volume is doubled?
skill: Explain behaviour and applications of gases under pressure
feedback: As volume decreases, pressure increases, meaning as the one doubles, the other halves.

subject: Science
q: An aerosol can is a mechanism designed to turn _____ into _____.
skill: Explain the working of aerosols
feedback: An aerosol can is a mechanism designed to turn a liquid, such as paint or polish, into a finely dispersed mist.

subject: Science
q: In _____ aerosols, the dominant component of the particulate matter comes from ammonium nitrate.
skill: Explain the working of aerosols
feedback: Nitrate aerosols are made in the atmosphere from ammonium nitrate. They are produced from volcanic and marine sources.

subject: Science
q:
What are the causes of an increase in aerosols in the atmosphere?

I.   plantation of trees
II.  pollution from cars
III. burning of plastic
skill: Explain the working of aerosols
feedback: We, humans, add aerosols to the atmosphere too. Aerosols are produced by pollution from cars, burning of plastic, power plants, and factories that burn fossil fuels.

subject: Science
q: Large aerosols are _____.
skill: Explain the working of aerosols
feedback: Large aerosols are visible but their size is still very small.

subject: Science
q: Some aerosols are invisible because they _____.
options: are too small to see with the naked eye
are smaller than the wavelength of light
skill: Explain the working of aerosols
feedback: Some aerosols are so small that they are made up of only a few molecules. They are invisible because they are smaller than the wavelength of light.

subject: Science
q: Steam is an example of aerosols, made up of _____.
skill: Explain the working of aerosols
feedback: Steam is water in the gas phase, which is formed when water boils. Steam is invisible; however, "steam" often refers to wet steam, the visible mist or aerosol of water droplets formed as this water vapour condenses.

subject: Science
q: Aerosols are also known as _____.
skill: Explain the working of aerosols
feedback: There are billions of tiny bits of solids and liquids floating in the atmosphere. Those tiny floating particles are called aerosols or particulates.

subject: Science
q:
Which are examples of aerosols?

I.   detergents
II.  vinegar
III. hairspray
skill: Explain the working of aerosols
feedback: Aerosols make it easy to paint, polish, and lubricate. They are used in lots of other household chemicals, from deodorants and hairsprays to air fresheners and detergents.

subject: Science
q:
What is the correct order of these celestial objects from the smallest to the largest?

1. Sun
2. Earth
3. Solar system
4. Milky Way
skill: Describe galaxies, types of galaxies, and constellations
feedback:
The Earth is a planet and is therefore smallest in size. The Sun is a star and is also bigger than Earth. Our solar system is even bigger as it includes the Sun and as well as its 8 planets. The Milky Way is a galaxy containing billions of stars like our Sun and is therefore the biggest in size.
Hence, the correct order is: 2 → 1 → 3 → 4

subject: Science
q: The image below shows the Leo constellation. Its stars are arranged like a _____ and a _____.
skill: Describe galaxies, types of galaxies, and constellations
feedback: Leo is a famous constellation. Its stars are arranged like a backward question mark and a triangle.

subject: Science
q: Which constellation is shown in the image below?
skill: Describe galaxies, types of galaxies, and constellations
feedback:
These five stars forming a capital M or W belong to the constellation Cassiopeia. Many believe that the star pattern looks like a queen sitting on her throne.
The Milky Way is a galaxy, not a constellation.

subject: Science
q: Which constellation is shown in the image below?
skill: Describe galaxies, types of galaxies, and constellations
feedback:
The seven stars in the image form the constellation named the Big Dipper. Four stars on one side make the bowl and the remaining three stars form its handle.
Leo and Cassiopeia are constellations with different patterns and numbers or stars. Andromeda is a galaxy, not a constellation.

subject: Science
q: You are the captain of a ship that is temporarily lost at sea at night. Which star will you look for to help with navigation?
skill: Describe galaxies, types of galaxies, and constellations
feedback:
As the captain of the stranded ship, you will look for the Big Dipper constellation as the Pole Star is located just north of the Big Dipper and helps in navigation at night. The Pole Star, also called the North Star, is important for navigation because it is located directly over the North Pole.
Wherever you are in the northern hemisphere, the North Star can help you navigate as well as find your latitude.

subject: Science
q: We live in the _____ galaxy, and it is a/an _____ galaxy.
skill: Describe galaxies, types of galaxies, and constellations
feedback: The Milky Way is our home galaxy. Andromeda is our neighbouring galaxy and both Milky Way and Andromeda are spiral galaxies.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following statements about spiral and elliptical galaxies is FALSE?
options: Spiral galaxies rotate on their axes, but elliptical galaxies do not.
Spiral galaxies are shaped like spirals, while elliptical galaxies are ovals.
Spiral galaxies contain new stars, whereas elliptical galaxies contain old stars.
Spiral galaxies contain less dust and gas compared to elliptical galaxies.
skill: Describe galaxies, types of galaxies, and constellations
feedback: Spiral galaxies have spiral arms. The rotate on their axes and contain new stars. Elliptical galaxies are oval, stationary, and contain only old stars because they have less dust and gas compared to spiral galaxies.

subject: Science
q: The image below shows the galaxy NGC 1427A. What type of galaxy is it?
skill: Describe galaxies, types of galaxies, and constellations
feedback: The image shows a galaxy without a definite shape. It is neither spiral nor oval. Therefore, NGC 1427A is an irregular galaxy.

subject: Science
q: Polaris is a _____ star because it is _____ and has a medium temperature compared to other stars. Beta is a _____ star because it is _____ and has the highest temperature.
skill: Define stars and explain the properties of stars
feedback:
The size and temperature of a star are related. A medium-sized star has a medium temperature. Medium-sized and medium temperature stars are yellow. Therefore, Polaris is a yellow star.

The bigger the star, the higher the temperature. A massive star has the highest temperature. This type of star is blue. Therefore, Beta is a blue star.

subject: Science
q: What is true about the stars shown in the image?
options: The red star is brighter than the orange star.
The orange star is hotter than the red star.
The red star is larger than the orange star
The orange star is farther away from the Earth than the red star.
skill: Define stars and explain the properties of stars
feedback:
Red stars are the coldest stars. Therefore, an orange star would be hotter than the red star. The orange star is also larger than the red star.
However, the colour of the star does not depend on its brightness or its distance from the Earth.

subject: Science
q: The image below shows 5 different stars, A-E. Which star is yellow and which one is blue?
options: yellow star = C; blue star = E
yellow star = A; blue star = B
yellow star = C; blue star = D
yellow star = D; blue star = A
skill: Define stars and explain the properties of stars
feedback:
The axes show the varying temperature and mass of stars A-E.
A yellow star is a medium-sized, medium temperature star. The star in the middle is C. Therefore, C is a yellow star.
The blue star is the hottest, most massive star. The star on the top with the highest temperature and mass is E. Therefore, E is a blue star.

subject: Science
q:
The colour of a star depends on which of the following factor(s)?

I.   mass
II.  shape
III. temperature
IV. distance from the Earth
skill: Define stars and explain the properties of stars
feedback:
The colour of a star depends on two factors:
1. The temperature of the star.
2. The mass of the star.

It does not depend on the shape of the star, nor its distance from the Earth.

subject: Science
q: A light year is the _____ light travels in one year at the speed of _____.
skill: Define stars and explain the properties of stars
feedback:
A light year is not a measure of time, it is a measure of distance.
Thus, a light year is the distance light travels in one year at the speed of light, which is 300,000 km/s.

subject: Science
q: The distance between stars is measured in
skill: Define stars and explain the properties of stars
feedback: The distance between stars is measured in light years. This is because these distances are too great to be measured in metres or kilometres.

subject: Science
q:
Which of the following statements is/are true about light years?

I.   A light year is the distance travelled by light in one year.
II.  One light year is equal to 9.5 trillion kilometres.
III. In space, time is measured in light years.
skill: Define stars and explain the properties of stars
feedback:
A light year is the distance travelled by light in one year. One light year is also equal to 9.5 trillion kilometres.
However, time in space is not measured in light years. Instead, the distances in space are measured in light years.
Therefore, only statements I and II are true and III is false.

subject: Science
q: Sara observes a stellar nebula and a planetary nebula. Which one is in a later stage of the star cycle?
options: Both are at the same stage
skill: Define stars and explain the life of stars
feedback:
A stellar nebula is the initial collection of gases and dust that leads to stars formation.
A planetary nebula forms around the core of a collapsed star. Thus, the planetary nebula is at a later stage of development.

subject: Science
q: Ali wants to directly observe a black hole? Why is not possible?
options: A black hole's pull of gravity is so strong that even light cannot escape it.
It emits light that is not visible to the human eye.
skill: Define stars and explain the life of stars
feedback: Black holes are so massive that the strong gravity does not let light escape. Without any light, the black hole appears black to Ali.

subject: Science
q: Ali observes two stars and wants to determine which will have a shorter life. What should Ali measure?
options: the amount of light emitted by both stars
the distance the stars are from Ali
the amount of heat emitted by both stars
skill: Define stars and explain the life of stars
feedback:
The full lifespan of a star is determined by its mass.
Stars with very large masses tend to complete their life cycle quicker. This is because the hydrogen in their core is used up faster than in less massive stars.

subject: Science
q: A star drastically increases in volume. What stage is the star at?
skill: Define stars and explain the life of stars
feedback: In the red giant stage, the star swells up and increases in size significantly. For this reason, stars in the red giant stage occupy a much larger volume than stars in a more stable state.

subject: Science
q: What will be the likely final state of our Sun?
skill: Define stars and explain the life of stars
feedback: Our Sun is an average-sized star compared to other stars and does not have enough mass to become a black hole. The Sun will likely become a red giant and end its life as a black dwarf.

subject: Science
q: Sania sees a star that is increasing in brightness and eventually emitting light that outshines its neighbouring stars significantly. What has Sania witnessed?
options: the formation of a red giant.
the formation of a black hole
the formation of a white dwarf
skill: Define stars and explain the life of stars
feedback: Supernovas are huge explosions that release immense amounts of heat and light that may even outshine stars across the entire galaxy.

subject: Science
q: What are the next stages after a star becomes a red giant or a red supergiant?
skill: Define stars and explain the life of stars
feedback:
Depending on its mass, a star will either turn into a planetary nebula or become a supernova.
A low-mass star turns into a red giant and its next stage is a planetary nebula.
On the other hand, a massive star becomes a red supergiant and its next stage is a supernova.

subject: Science
q: Why must space suits be thermally insulated?
options: to protect astronauts against extreme temperatures
to protect astronauts from free-moving objects
to protect astronauts from the harmful radiation of the Sun
to allow astronauts to breathe oxygen
skill: Identify the advances in technology due to space exploration
feedback: The temperatures in space vary widely. It is extremely cold in some areas and extremely hot in areas directly exposed to the Sun. Therefore, space suits must be thermally insulated <b>to protect astronauts against these extreme temperatures.

subject: Science
q: _____ is/are added to freeze-dried food before it is consumed by astronauts.
skill: Identify the advances in technology due to space exploration
feedback: Astronauts' food is transported and stored in specialised packaging. The food is freeze-dried to make it easier to store. The food can be eaten after water is added to it.

subject: Science
q: Astronauts have devices in their helmets which convert sound waves into _____ to enable them to communicate.
skill: Identify the advances in technology due to space exploration
feedback: Sound cannot travel in space as there is no air. Therefore, to communicate, astronauts have devices that convert sound into radio waves. These waves are then relayed to another astronaut or back to Earth.

subject: Science
q: Why was memory foam originally invented?
options: to lessen the impact in aircraft seats during landing
to allow astronauts in space to sleep more comfortably
to allow people with back problems to sleep more comfortably
to lessen the impact in bicycle seats during a collision
skill: Identify the advances in technology due to space exploration
feedback: Memory foam is used commonly in mattresses to make sleeping more comfortable. However, it was originally invented to lessen the impact in aircraft seats during landing.

subject: Science
q: Why was a cordless, handheld drill originally invented?
options: to drill holes in the walls of houses
to penetrate the soil on Earth to check for water
to obtain rock samples from the Moon
to drill holes into space shuttles for temperature regulation
skill: Identify the advances in technology due to space exploration
feedback: Cordless drills are commonly used in construction. For example, they are used to drill holes in the walls of houses. However, they were originally invented by scientists to allow astronomers to obtain rock samples from the Moon.

subject: Science
q: Scratch-resistant material is commonly used in the lenses of glasses. Why was this material originally invented?
options: to ensure that the surface of space shuttles is not scratched during re-entry into Earth
to protect the mirrors of the Hubble Space Telescope from being damaged
to make the cameras used by astronauts more durable
to coat the helmets of the space suits worn by astronauts
skill: Identify the advances in technology due to space exploration
feedback: When astronauts first started going to the Moon, scientists wanted to ensure that the space suits they wore were tough and long-lasting. One of the steps they took was to coat the helmets of these suits with scratch-resistant material.

subject: Science
q: Freeze-dried food originally created for astronauts in space is used all over the world. Why?
options: People who like space travel buy this food.
Freeze-dried food is easy to store.
Freeze-dried food is healthier than normal food.
skill: Identify the advances in technology due to space exploration
feedback: Freeze-dried food occupies a small amount of space as all the water content is removed. This makes it easy to store. To eat such food, hot water has to be added to it.

subject: Science
q: Space engineers originally created shock absorbers for the shoes of astronauts on the Moon. This technology is now used in athletic shoes. This is an example of
options: an unplanned benefit of space exploration.
a planned benefit of space exploration.
a known risk of space exploration.
an unknown risk of space exploration.
skill: Identify the advances in technology due to space exploration
feedback: Space engineers invented a technology that created hollow shoe soles, which were then filled with shock-absorbent rubber. This same technology is now commonly used in athletic shoes. As the purpose of the original technology was not focused on athletics, this is an example of an unplanned benefit of space exploration.

subject: Science
q: While viewing a distant object from a refracting telescope, why does the image appear bigger than the object?
options: The eyepiece brings the image from the principal focus and magnifies its size.
The eyepiece brings the image from the objective lens and magnifies its size.
The objective lens brings the image from the eye piece and magnifies its size.
Parallel rays of light directly hit the objective lens where they are magnified.
skill: Explain the working of a telescope
feedback: Refracting telescopes contain two convex lenses. Convex lenses are thicker in the middle than they are at the edges. One lens is called the objective lens and the other is the eyepiece lens. They are both fixed in a long tube. Light comes in from a distant object, enters the objective lens and refracts to a point called the principal focus. The eyepiece brings the image from the principal focus and magnifies it to the size of the eye's pupil. This virtual image appears closer to our eyes than the real object, which is why it looks bigger than the object.

subject: Science
q: Predict the principle behind the working of a reflecting telescope.
options: A concave mirror is used to converge light on principal focus.
A convex mirror is used to converge light on the principal focus.
A concave lens is used to converge light on the principal focus.
A convex lens is used to converge light on the principal focus.
skill: Explain the working of a telescope
feedback: In a reflecting telescope, instead of a convex objective lens, a concave mirror is used to diverge light on the principal focus. However, there is a smaller plane mirror that directs the incoming light rays to the eyepiece. Then the convex eyepiece lens is used to magnify this image for the viewer. Concave mirrors focus light at a focal point using reflection, not refraction.

subject: Science
q: Which instrument(s) can be used to look at the Sun?
options: A telescope and binoculars can be used.
A periscope and a microscope can be used.
Binoculars and a periscope can be used.
A telescope and a microscope can be used.
skill: Explain the working of a telescope
feedback: To look at the Sun, we can use a telescope or binoculars to project an image of the Sun onto a white screen.

subject: Science
q: Why can't we look at the Sun directly with the naked eye or through any unfiltered devices?
options: The heat from the Sun can damage the skin.
The light rays can damage the skin.
The light rays can damage the eyes.
A blurred image of the Sun will be formed.
skill: Explain the working of a telescope
feedback: The Sun is far away from the Earth but it can still harm our eyes. The strong light rays from the Sun can cause serious damage to our eyes if we stare directly at it.

subject: Science
q: Which type of mirror is used in a reflecting telescope?
skill: Explain the working of a telescope
feedback: In a reflecting telescope, instead of a convex objective lens, a concave mirror is used to collect parallel rays from an object to form an image at the principal focus. However, there is a smaller plane mirror that directs the incoming light rays to the eyepiece. Then the convex eyepiece is used to magnify this image for the viewer. Concave mirrors focus light at a focal point using reflection, not refraction.

subject: Science
q: Identify the type of image this telescope forms.
options: It forms a magnified real image.
It forms a magnified virtual image.
It forms a diminished real image.
It forms a diminished virtual image.
skill: Explain the working of a telescope
feedback: A refracting telescope uses an objective lens and an eyepiece to gather more light than the human eye is able to collect on its own. The two convex lenses focus light and a brighter, clearer, magnified virtual image is formed.

subject: Science
q: The method below is used to view the Sun through an optical device. Identify the device and describe why the Sun is viewed like this.
options: The device is a telescope and it is used like this to get information about the size of the Sun.
The device is a periscope and it is used like this to protect the eyes from the strong rays of the Sun.
The device is a telescope and it is used like this to protect the eyes from the strong rays of the Sun.
The device is a periscope and it is used like this to get a clear image of the Sun.
skill: Explain the working of a telescope
feedback: The Sun is too far away from the Earth but it can still cause serious eye damage. The strong light rays from the Sun harm our eyes if we stare directly at it. To look at the Sun, we can use a telescope or binoculars to project an image of the Sun onto a white screen.

subject: Science
q: Choose the principle behind the working of a refracting telescope.
options: Two convex mirrors are used to converge light on the principal focus.
Two convex lenses are used to converge light on the principal focus.
Two concave mirrors are used to converge light on the principal focus.
Two concave lenses are used to converge light on the principal focus.
skill: Explain the working of a telescope
feedback: A refracting telescope uses an objective lens and an eyepiece to gather more light than the human eye is able to collect on its own. The two convex lenses focus light and a brighter, clearer, magnified virtual image is formed.

subject: Science
q: Choose the correct principle that shows the working of a refracting telescope.
options: Two convex lenses are used to focus light and create a magnified and virtual image.
Two mirrors are used to focus light and create a magnified and virtual image.
Two concave lenses are used to focus light and create a magnified and virtual image.
The receiver receives light rays and converts them into electrical signals.
skill: Discuss the telescopes and spectrometers used in space exploration
feedback:
A refracting telescope uses an objective lens and an eyepiece to gather more light than the human eye is able to collect on its own. Both these lenses are convex lenses that work together to focus light and form a magnified and virtual image.

Mirrors are used by reflecting telescopes. Concave lenses are not used in telescopes.

subject: Science
q: The diagram below shows a reflecting telescope. Which part of the telescope will incoming light from a distant object strike first?
skill: Discuss the telescopes and spectrometers used in space exploration
feedback: All objects in space are so far away that all of the light rays coming from them reach Earth as parallel rays. After entering the telescope, these rays first strike the front part of the primary mirror, which is concave in shape. This part is shown by III in the diagram. The light goes on to a hit a secondary mirror, labelled as II. It finally goes through the eyepiece, labelled as I.

subject: Science
q:
Three telescopes are listed below.

I. reflecting telescope
II. radio telescope
III. refracting telescope

Which of these is/are optical?
skill: Discuss the telescopes and spectrometers used in space exploration
feedback: There are two types of telescopes: optical and non-optical telescopes. Optical telescopes have further subtypes called reflecting telescopes and refracting telescopes. These telescopes gather visible light and use lenses or mirrors to form images. Non-optical telescopes like radio telescopes detect non-visible radiation.

subject: Science
q: Why should the objective lens of a telescope have a large aperture?
options: to gather a large amount of light
to form a real and inverted image of the object
To form a virtual and small image of the object
to observe distant objects on cloudy days
skill: Discuss the telescopes and spectrometers used in space exploration
feedback: The aperture of a telescope is the diameter of the light-collecting region. For example, in a reflecting telescope, the size of the primary mirror determines the size of the aperture. A large aperture means the telescope can gather a large amount of light.

subject: Science
q: A telescope that uses convex lenses to make distant objects seem closer is known as a
skill: Discuss the telescopes and spectrometers used in space exploration
feedback: A refracting telescope (also called a refractor) uses an objective lens and an eyepiece to gather light. Both these lenses are convex lenses. They work together to focus light and form a bright, clear, and magnified image of a distant object.

subject: Science
q: The diagram below shows parts of a radio telescope. Which part converts radio waves into electrical signals?
skill: Discuss the telescopes and spectrometers used in space exploration
feedback: A radio telescope is an instrument consisting of a radio receiver and an antenna system that is used to detect radio waves emitted by bodies from outer space. The receiver takes radio waves received by the antenna and converts them to electrical signals. The part labelled I in the diagram is the radio receiver.

subject: Science
q: Which part of the electromagnetic spectrum do optical telescopes detect?
skill: Discuss the telescopes and spectrometers used in space exploration
feedback: An optical telescope gathers and focuses light mainly from the visible part of the electromagnetic spectrum. Visible objects are things that we can see. Optical telescopes increase the size of distant objects as well as their brightness.
